analysis.justification: A1 receives the encrypted message {|K6,A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3) from a3 using their shared key shk(A1,a3). Since only a3 and A1 know this key, A1 can verify the message originated from a3. The message contains K6 and A2, so A1 can be confident a3 intended to send these values. However, there's no nonce from A1 in this encryption to guarantee freshness, which slightly reduces confidence.

A2 receives {|K6,A1,A2,N5|}shk(A2,a3) from A1, not directly from a3. An attacker could replay a previous message from A1 to A2. Since A2 cannot verify whether A1 received this from a3 in the current session, A2 cannot authenticate a3 on these values. The attacker could intercept and forward old messages without a3's participation.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_1; A1 -> a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_1; a3 -> A1: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3),{|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3); A1 -> A2: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3)
Session 2: Attacker replays {|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3) from Session 1 to A2 without a3's participation in Session 2. A2 accepts this as fresh authentication from a3.

analysis.justification: K6 is always encrypted under shk(A1,a3) or shk(A2,a3) when transmitted. These are pre-shared keys known only to the respective pairs. The Dolev-Yao attacker cannot decrypt these messages without knowing either shared key. Since both A1-A3 and A2-A3 channels protect K6, it remains secret among the three parties.

A1 accepts K6 based solely on decrypting {|K6,A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3), but N4 is chosen by A1 and not guaranteed fresh to a3 (a3 does not contribute a nonce). An attacker can replay an old a3->A1 message from a prior run where the same N4 value occurs (or where A1 reuses N4), making A1 believe a3 is participating now. This breaks injective/non-injective agreement depending on freshness assumptions; at minimum, replay can violate agreement on the run. The protocol lacks an explicit a3-generated challenge or timestamp to bind a3’s liveness to the current run.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2->A1: A1,A2,N5_1; A1->a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_1; a3->A1: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3),{|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3).
Session 2: A2->A1: A1,A2,N5_2; A1->a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_2 (A1 repeats N4_1); I(replay)->A1: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3),{|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3) (replayed from Session 1); A1 accepts K6_1 as from a3 for A2.

analysis.justification: A2’s only evidence of a3 is receiving {|K6,A1,A2,N5|}shk(A2,a3), but this ciphertext can be replayed from an earlier run with the same N5. Since N5 is generated by A2, if N5 is ever reused (or predictable), an attacker can replay an old ticket and make A2 accept an old K6 without a3 participating in the current run. Also, A2 cannot tell whether the ticket came directly from a3 or was forwarded by A1/attacker, so agreement on the peer run is weak. Lack of explicit session identifiers beyond N5 enables replay-based authentication failure.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2->A1: A1,A2,N5_1; A1->a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_1; a3->A1: ...,{|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3); A1->A2: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3) (A2 accepts K6_1).
Session 2: A2->A1: A1,A2,N5_1 (N5 reused); I->A2: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3) (replay from Session 1); A2 accepts K6_1 believing a3 is involved in Session 2 though a3 did not run.

analysis.justification: K6 is only ever transmitted inside symmetric encryptions under shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3). Under Dolev-Yao assumptions, the attacker cannot decrypt these without the corresponding shared keys, and those keys are only in the initial knowledge of the intended principals. Even with replay, the attacker learns no new information about K6 because ciphertexts remain opaque. Therefore confidentiality of K6 against an external network attacker holds in this model.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I observes A2->A1: A1,A2,N5_1; I observes A1->a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_1; I observes a3->A1: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3),{|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3); I cannot derive K6_1.
Session 2: I replays observed ciphertexts to A1/A2; despite causing acceptance/reuse, I still cannot decrypt {|K6_1,...|}shk(*) and cannot derive K6_1.

    },

      "goal_id": 3,

Since these symmetric keys are only shared between a3 and each respective agent, and the attacker lacks both shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), K6 remains confidential. analysis.justification: A1 receives K6 and A2 inside a symmetric encryption under shk(A1,a3), which is only known to A1 and a3. Since the attacker cannot forge this encryption without shk(A1,a3), A1 can be confident that a3 generated this message. The inclusion of A2 in the encrypted payload ensures A1 knows a3 intended A2 as the other party.
analysis.two_session_trace: Session 1: A1(A1), A2(A2), a3(a3) execute normally. Session 2: A1(A1'), A2(A2'), a3(a3') execute normally with different nonces.

analysis.justification: A2 receives {|K6,A1,A2,N5|}shk(A2,a3) from A1, not directly from a3. An attacker could replay this message from a previous session where A2 played the same role. Since A2 cannot verify freshness beyond N5 (which A2 originally generated), there is no guarantee that a3 is currently involved or that K6 is fresh. This violates authentication as A2 cannot be sure a3 agrees on the current session.
analysis.two_session_trace: Session 1: A1(A1), A2(A2), a3(a3) execute fully, producing {|K6,A1,A2,N5|}shk(A2,a3). Session 2: Attacker replays that message to A2(A2) without a3's involvement, making A2 believe a3 sent a fresh K6.

analysis.justification: K6 is generated by a3 and sent encrypted under shk(A1,a3) to A1 and under shk(A2,a3) to A2. Since these symmetric keys are only shared between a3 and each respective agent, and the attacker lacks both shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), K6 remains confidential. No other messages reveal K6 in plaintext or under keys the attacker knows.
analysis.two_session_trace: Session 1: A1(A1), A2(A2), a3(a3) execute normally. Session 2: A1(A1'), A2(A2'), a3(a3') execute normally with different K6'.

analysis.justification: A1 accepts K6 based solely on a message encrypted under shk(A1,a3), but the protocol provides no evidence that a3 intended K6 specifically for A2 beyond plaintext fields inside that encryption. An attacker can replay an old a3->A1 ciphertext from a different run where the embedded A2 matches, causing A1 to accept without a fresh corresponding a3 run for the current N4. Since A1 never gets a3’s response bound to N5 (A2’s nonce) under shk(A1,a3), A1 cannot ensure a3’s participation for this session’s peer A2.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_1; A1 -> a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_1; a3 -> A1: C1={|K6_1,A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3), C2={|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3) (attacker records C1).
Session 2: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_2; A1 -> a3: A1,A2,N4_2,N5_2 (attacker blocks); attacker -> A1: replay C1 from Session 1; A1 accepts K6_1 as from a3 for A2 without a matching a3 run for N4_2.

analysis.justification: A2 accepts K6 when it receives {|K6,A1,A2,N5|}shk(A2,a3), but this ciphertext can be replayed by an attacker from an earlier run with the same N5 value. Because N5 is chosen by A2, if N5 repeats across sessions (or is predictable/low-entropy), A2 cannot distinguish a fresh a3 response from a replay. Additionally, A2 never sees N4, so it cannot check linkage to A1’s contribution, weakening agreement on the session context.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_r; A1 -> a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_r; a3 -> A1: C2_r={|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_r|}shk(A2,a3); A1 -> A2: C2_r (attacker records C2_r).
Session 2: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_r (nonce repeats); attacker -> A2: replay C2_r directly (or via A1); A2 decrypts with shk(A2,a3) and accepts K6_1 as from a3 for A1 without a fresh a3 run.

analysis.justification: K6 is only ever transmitted inside symmetric encryptions under shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3). Under Dolev-Yao assumptions, the attacker cannot decrypt these without the corresponding shared keys, and no other message reveals K6 in the clear or under attacker-known keys. Replay does not help derive K6; it only reuses ciphertexts. Thus confidentiality of K6 against the network attacker holds given uncompromised shared keys.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker observes a3 -> A1: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3), {|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3) and A1 -> A2: {|K6_1,A1,A2,N5_1|}shk(A2,a3); attacker cannot decrypt.
Session 2: Attacker repeats observation/replay of the same ciphertexts or fresh ones; without shk(A1,a3) or shk(A2,a3), attacker still cannot derive K6.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0074

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4,N5;
Symmetric_key K6;
Function [Number -> Number] pre;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,pre,shk(A1,a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,pre,shk(A2,a3);
a3: A1,A2,a3,pre,shk(A1,a3),shk(A2,a3);
A1,a3 share shk(A1,a3);
A2,a3 share shk(A2,a3)
Actions:
A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5
A1 -> a3: A1,A2,N4,N5
a3 -> A1: {|K6,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3),{|K6,A1,N5,a3|}shk(A2,a3)
A1 -> A2: {|K6,A1,N5,a3|}shk(A2,a3)

GOALS:
1. A1 authenticates a3 on K6,A2
2. A2 authenticates a3 on K6,A1
3. K6 secret between A1,A2,a3

analysis.justification: A1 receives {|K6,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3) from a3, encrypted with their shared symmetric key shk(A1,a3). Since only A1 and a3 know this key, A1 can verify the message originated from a3. The inclusion of N4 (fresh nonce from A1) and A2 in the encrypted payload ensures a3 is authenticating on these specific data items. The attacker cannot forge this message without shk(A1,a3).

analysis.justification: A2 receives {|K6,A1,N5,a3|}shk(A2,a3) from A1, not directly from a3. While this is encrypted with shk(A2,a3), A2 cannot determine whether A1 is honestly forwarding a3's message or replaying/modifying an old message. A1 could replay a previous session's message from a3 to A2, making A2 believe a3 is authenticating on fresh K6,A1 when a3 is not involved in the current session. This breaks authentication as A2 cannot verify a3's recent participation.
analysis.two_session_trace: Session 1: A1 and A2 complete normal protocol with a3. Session 2: A1 replays the encrypted blob {|K6,A1,N5,a3|}shk(A2,a3) from Session 1 to A2 without contacting a3. A2 accepts it as fresh authentication from a3, but a3 is not involved.

analysis.justification: K6 is generated by a3 and sent encrypted under shk(A1,a3) to A1 and under shk(A2,a3) to A2 (via A1). These shared keys are not known to other agents. The attacker cannot decrypt these messages without compromising either shk(A1,a3) or shk(A2,a3). K6 never appears in plaintext or under keys known to the attacker. However, if A1 is compromised, they could leak K6 to outsiders, but that is outside the Dolev-Yao model.
analysis.two_session_trace: Session 1: a3 generates K6, encrypts it for A1 and A2 using their respective shared keys. Session 2: Different K6 generated, similarly protected. Attacker intercepts all messages but cannot decrypt to learn K6.

analysis.justification: A1 accepts K6 and A2 based solely on a ciphertext under shk(A1,a3), but the protocol provides no freshness/authentication evidence that a3 generated this ciphertext for the current run or in response to A1’s N4,N5. An attacker can replay an old a3->A1 message (first component) from a prior run; A1 will decrypt it and believe it is from a3 and bound to A2 even though a3 is not participating now. The presence of N4 inside the ciphertext does not help if the attacker replays a matching old run where N4 was the same value as in the current run (possible under symbolic nonces or if N4 is reused/guessable). Thus non-injective agreement fails due to replay.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_1; A1 -> a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_1; a3 -> A1: C1={|K6_1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3), C2={|K6_1,A1,N5_1,a3|}shk(A2,a3).
Session 2: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_2; A1 -> a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_2 (attacker forces reuse/chooses N4_1); attacker replays to A1 the old C1 from Session 1; A1 decrypts and accepts K6_1,A2 as from a3 without a3 participating in Session 2.

analysis.justification: A2’s only evidence about a3 is the ciphertext {|K6,A1,N5,a3|}shk(A2,a3) received from A1, but A2 cannot tell whether it was freshly produced by a3 for this run or replayed. An attacker can replay an old ciphertext created by a3 under shk(A2,a3) that matches A2’s current N5, causing A2 to accept K6 and believe a3 participated. Since A2 does not contribute any value that a3 signs/encrypts uniquely for the run beyond N5, and there is no injective binding to A1’s run, replay breaks authentication.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_1; A1 -> a3: A1,A2,N4_1,N5_1; a3 -> A1: C1={|K6_1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3), C2={|K6_1,A1,N5_1,a3|}shk(A2,a3); A1 -> A2: C2.
Session 2: A2 -> A1: A1,A2,N5_1 (attacker replays/forces reuse of N5_1); attacker sends to A2 the old C2 from Session 1 (possibly via A1 or directly); A2 decrypts with shk(A2,a3) and accepts K6_1,A1,a3 as if a3 participated in Session 2.

analysis.justification: K6 is only ever transmitted inside symmetric encryptions under shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are initially known only to the respective endpoints and a3. Under Dolev-Yao assumptions, the attacker cannot decrypt these ciphertexts without the shared keys, and no message reveals shk(.) or K6 in the clear. Even with replay, the attacker learns no new information about K6 beyond ciphertexts. Therefore confidentiality of K6 against the network attacker holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:

analysis.justification: The goal requires that A1 sends N4 to A2 on an insecure channel. In the protocol body, the first message 'A1 -> A2: N4' directly satisfies this goal. The Dolev-Yao attacker can observe this message since it's sent on an insecure channel, but cannot prevent its delivery. The subsequent messages don't affect the basic fact that N4 was transmitted from A1 to A2. No replay or manipulation attack prevents this initial transmission from occurring as specified.

Session 1: A1 generates N4 and sends it to A2 via insecure channel. A2 receives N4.

Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker can intercept and learn N4 immediately, violating confidentiality. Subsequent messages do not restore secrecy because the attacker already knows N4. Therefore the goal is broken in any execution where the first message occurs.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: N4 ; I intercepts and learns N4.
Session 2: A1 -> A2: N4' ; I intercepts and learns N4'.

An attacker who obtains inv(pk(A1)) or inv(pk(A2)) can decrypt any intercepted message containing N5.",
            "two_session_trace": "Session 1: A1 -> A2: {N4_1,A2}pk(A2); A2 -> A1: {N4_1,A1}pk(A1); A1 -> A2: {N5_1,A2}pk(A2); A2 -> A1: {N5_1,A1}pk(A1)\nSession 2: A1 -> A2: {N4_2,A2}pk(A2); A2 -> A1: {N4_2,A1}pk(A1); A1 -> A2: {N5_2,A2}pk(A2); A2 -> A1: {N5_2,A1}pk(A1)"

analysis.justification: N4 is transmitted in plaintext within asymmetric encryption in messages 1 and 2. While encrypted with public keys, the Dolev-Yao attacker can intercept both messages and decrypt them using the corresponding private keys if they compromise either A1 or A2. Since the protocol doesn't establish fresh session keys and reuses long-term keys, compromise of either agent's private key reveals N4. Additionally, N4 lacks freshness guarantees and could be replayed from previous sessions.

N5 suffers from the same vulnerability as N4 - it's transmitted in plaintext within asymmetric encryption in messages 3 and 4. The encryption uses long-term public keys, so compromise of either agent's private key reveals N5. The protocol provides no forward secrecy, and N5 is not bound to any fresh session-specific values. An attacker who obtains inv(pk(A1)) or inv(pk(A2)) can decrypt any intercepted message containing N5.

N4 is sent encrypted under pk(A2), so passive eavesdroppers cannot learn it. However, there is no authentication of public keys, so an active attacker can substitute pk(A2) with pk(I) and obtain {N4,A2}pk(I), then decrypt to learn N4. The later message {N4,A1}pk(A1) does not prevent this because it is also unauthenticated and can be forged or relayed. Thus secrecy of N4 between A1 and A2 fails under Dolev-Yao with key-substitution (MITM).

Session 1: A1 -> I(A2): {N4,A2}pk(I) (I substituted pk(A2)); I decrypts with inv(pk(I)) and learns N4; I -> A2: {N4,A2}pk(A2) (re-encrypt/forward).
Session 2: A2 -> A1: {N4,A1}pk(A1) (normal response); I observes but cannot decrypt; secrecy already broken from Session 1.

analysis.justification: N5 is also only protected by encryption to pk(A2) without any mechanism to authenticate that pk(A2) is truly A2’s key. An active attacker can perform the same key-substitution attack as for N4, causing A1 to encrypt N5 to the attacker’s public key and revealing N5 upon decryption. The final message {N5,A1}pk(A1) does not restore secrecy because the attacker already learned N5 earlier. Therefore N5 is not secret between A1 and A2 in the presence of an active network attacker.

Session 2: A2 -> A1: {N5,A1}pk(A1) (normal response); I observes but cannot decrypt; secrecy already broken from Session 1.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

A2: A2,A1,f,shk(A1,A2);
A1,A2 share shk(A1,A2)

A2 -> A1: A2,{|f(N3)|}K5

GOALS:

2. A2 *-> A1: N4

```json
{
  "model": "symbolic",
  "protocol_id": "PROTO_0077",
  "analysis": [
    {
      "goal_id": 1,
      "goal": "A1 *->* A2: N3",
      "status": "no attack",
      "confidence": 85,
      "justification": "The protocol provides both confidentiality and authentication for N3. In message 2, N3 is encrypted with the pre-shared symmetric key shk(A1,A2), which provides confidentiality since only A1 and A2 possess this key. Authentication is achieved because A1 includes its identity A1 in the encrypted payload, and the encryption itself provides message integrity. The responder A2 can verify the origin by decrypting with shk(A1,A2) and seeing A1's identity, confirming A1 as the source of N3.",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: A2 -> A1: A2,N4_1; A1 -> A2: A1,{|f(N4_1),N3_1,A1,K5_1|}shk(A1,A2); A2 -> A1: A2,{|f(N3_1)|}K5_1\nSession 2: A2 -> A1: A2,N4_2; A1 -> A2: A1,{|f(N4_2),N3_2,A1,K5_2|}shk(A1,A2); A2 -> A1: A2,{|f(N3_2)|}K5_2"
    },
    {
      "goal_id": 2,
      "goal": "A2 *-> A1: N4",
      "status": "attack found",
      "confidence": 90,
      "justification": "The first message A2 -> A1: A2,N4 is sent on an insecure channel without any authentication mechanism. An attacker can intercept this message and replay it later, causing A1 to believe A2 is initiating a new session with the same N4. Since there's no freshness guarantee or authentication in this initial message, A1 cannot verify whether N4 actually originated from A2 in the current session or is a replay from a previous session.",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: A2 -> A1: A2,N4_1 (legitimate)\nSession 2: Attacker replays message from Session 1: Attacker -> A1: A2,N4_1; A1 -> Attacker: A1,{|f(N4_1),N3_2,A1,K5_2|}shk(A1,A2); Attacker cannot proceed further but authentication of N4 has already been violated"
    }
  ]
}
```

analysis.justification: The protocol provides both confidentiality and authentication for N3. In message 2, N3 is encrypted with the pre-shared symmetric key shk(A1,A2), which provides confidentiality since only A1 and A2 possess this key. Authentication is achieved because A1 includes its identity A1 in the encrypted payload, and the encryption itself provides message integrity. The responder A2 can verify the origin by decrypting with shk(A1,A2) and seeing A1's identity, confirming A1 as the source of N3.

The first message A2 -> A1: A2,N4 is sent on an insecure channel without any authentication mechanism. An attacker can intercept this message and replay it later, causing A1 to believe A2 is initiating a new session with the same N4. Since there's no freshness guarantee or authentication in this initial message, A1 cannot verify whether N4 actually originated from A2 in the current session or is a replay from a previous session.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: A2,N4_1 (legitimate)
Session 2: Attacker replays message from Session 1: Attacker -> A1: A2,N4_1; A1 -> Attacker: A1,{|f(N4_1),N3_2,A1,K5_2|}shk(A1,A2); Attacker cannot proceed further but authentication of N4 has already been violated

analysis.justification: N3 is sent only inside {|f(N4),N3,A1,K5|}shk(A1,A2), so confidentiality against an outside attacker holds. However, the protocol does not bind A2's acceptance of N3 to a fresh, unique run: an attacker can replay A1's second message from an earlier run to A2 after eliciting the same N4 from A2, causing A2 to accept an old N3 as if it were current. This breaks injective agreement/secure-channel style delivery for N3 (replay).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2->A1: (A2,N4=x). A1->A2: (A1,{|f(x),N3=n_old,A1,K5=k_old|}shk). A2->A1: (A2,{|f(n_old)|}k_old).
Session 2: Attacker starts new run with A2: A2->I(A1): (A2,N4=x) (reused or attacker delays until same x appears). I replays to A2 the old message: (A1,{|f(x),N3=n_old,A1,K5=k_old|}shk). A2 accepts N3=n_old as from A1 in this run and responds (A2,{|f(n_old)|}k_old).

analysis.justification: The first message A2->A1: (A2,N4) is sent in clear on an insecure channel and carries no authentication (no MAC/signature, no secure channel). An attacker can forge this message to A1 with any chosen N4 while claiming sender A2, so A1 cannot authenticate that N4 originated from A2. This violates authenticated-channel style delivery for N4.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest A2->A1: (A2,N4=x). A1 proceeds normally.
Session 2: Attacker forges to A1: (A2,N4=y) with y chosen by attacker. A1 believes it received N4=y from A2 and sends (A1,{|f(y),N3=n2,A1,K5=k2|}shk) to A2, despite A2 never sending y.

goals_extracted: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0079

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Number N3,N4;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,pk,inv(pk(A1)),A2;
A2: A2,pk,inv(pk(A2)),A1
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {N3,A1}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {N3,N4,A2}pk(A1)
A1 -> A2: {N4}pk(A2)

GOALS:
1. A1 *->* A2: N3
2. A2 *->* A1: N4

models.raw_response_text:
```json
{
  "model": "symbolic",
  "protocol_id": "PROTO_0079",
  "analysis": [
    {
      "goal_id": 1,
      "goal": "A1 *->* A2: N3",
      "status": "attack found",
      "confidence": 95,
      "justification": "The protocol does not provide authentication of N3 from A1 to A2. In the first message, A1 sends {N3,A1}pk(A2), but A2 cannot verify the freshness or origin of N3 since anyone could replay this encrypted message. The encryption only provides confidentiality, not authentication of the sender. An attacker can intercept and replay the first message, making A2 believe A1 sent N3 when A1 did not initiate the protocol.",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: A1 -> A2: {N3_1,A1}pk(A2) (intercepted by attacker)\nSession 2: Attacker replays {N3_1,A1}pk(A2) to A2, A2 responds with {N3_1,N4_2,A2}pk(A1), attacker cannot decrypt but A2 believes A1 sent N3_1"
    },
    {
      "goal_id": 2,
      "goal": "A2 *->* A1: N4",
      "status": "attack found",
      "confidence": 95,
      "justification": "The protocol does not provide authentication of N4 from A2 to A1. In the second message, A2 sends {N3,N4,A2}pk(A1), but A1 cannot verify that N4 is fresh or originated from A2. An attacker could replay a captured second message, making A1 believe A2 sent N4 when A2 did not participate. The encryption provides confidentiality but not sender authentication, and there's no challenge-response mechanism to bind N4 to the current session.",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: A1 -> A2: {N3_1,A1}pk(A2), A2 -> A1: {N3_1,N4_1,A2}pk(A1) (intercepted by attacker)\nSession 2: Attacker initiates with A1 using captured {N3_1,A1}pk(A2), then replays {N3_1,N4_1,A2}pk(A1) to A1, making A1 believe A2 sent N4_1"
    }
  ]
}
```

analysis.justification: The protocol does not provide authentication of N3 from A1 to A2. In the first message, A1 sends {N3,A1}pk(A2), but A2 cannot verify the freshness or origin of N3 since anyone could replay this encrypted message. The encryption only provides confidentiality, not authentication of the sender. An attacker can intercept and replay the first message, making A2 believe A1 sent N3 when A1 did not initiate the protocol.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: {N3_1,A1}pk(A2) (intercepted by attacker)
Session 2: Attacker replays {N3_1,A1}pk(A2) to A2, A2 responds with {N3_1,N4_2,A2}pk(A1), attacker cannot decrypt but A2 believes A1 sent N3_1

analysis.justification: The protocol does not provide authentication of N4 from A2 to A1. In the second message, A2 sends {N3,N4,A2}pk(A1), but A1 cannot verify that N4 is fresh or originated from A2. An attacker could replay a captured second message, making A1 believe A2 sent N4 when A2 did not participate. The encryption provides confidentiality but not sender authentication, and there's no challenge-response mechanism to bind N4 to the current session.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: {N3_1,A1}pk(A2), A2 -> A1: {N3_1,N4_1,A2}pk(A1) (intercepted by attacker)
Session 2: Attacker initiates with A1 using captured {N3_1,A1}pk(A2), then replays {N3_1,N4_1,A2}pk(A1) to A1, making A1 believe A2 sent N4_1

analysis.justification: N3 is freshly chosen by A1 and only ever sent encrypted under pk(A2), so the attacker cannot learn it without inv(pk(A2)). A2’s receipt of N3 is tied to A1’s identity inside the ciphertext {N3,A1}pk(A2), preventing unknown-key-share style confusion about who generated N3. Replays of message 1 do not let the attacker learn N3, and do not create a second distinct N3 value. Under Dolev-Yao with uncompromised keys, confidentiality of N3 between A1 and A2 holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1->A2: {N3,A1}pk(A2); attacker forwards unchanged; A2 decrypts with inv(pk(A2)) and obtains N3.
Session 2: attacker replays Session1 msg1 to A2; A2 may accept same N3 again, but attacker still cannot derive N3 and no new secret is exposed.

analysis.justification: N4 is generated by A2 and is only transmitted encrypted under pk(A1) in message 2 and under pk(A2) in message 3, so the attacker cannot derive N4 without inv(pk(A1)) or inv(pk(A2)). A1 learns N4 only by decrypting {N3,N4,A2}pk(A1), which also binds N4 to A2’s claimed identity and to the session’s N3. Even if an attacker replays old message 2, it does not reveal N4 to the attacker; it only risks A1 accepting an old N4, which is not a confidentiality break. Therefore secrecy/confidentiality of N4 between A2 and A1 holds in this model.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1->A2: {N3,A1}pk(A2); A2->A1: {N3,N4,A2}pk(A1); attacker forwards; A1 decrypts and obtains N4.
Session 2: attacker replays Session1 msg2 to A1 without involving A2; A1 may accept old N4, but attacker still cannot decrypt and cannot learn N4.

An attacker could replay a previous message 3 from A1 to A2, making A2 believe A1 is freshly authenticating when A1 is not participating. The protocol lacks freshness mechanisms like nonces or timestamps to prevent replay attacks.

analysis.justification: A1 receives N5 encrypted under pk(A1) in message 6, but an attacker could replay a previous message 6 from A2 to A1. analysis.justification: N4 is always encrypted under pk(A2) when transmitted (message 3) and under pk(A1) in message 6. The attacker cannot decrypt these messages without inv(pk(A2)) or inv(pk(A1)).

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0081

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Number N3,N4;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,pk,inv(pk(A1)),A2;
A2: A2,pk,inv(pk(A2))
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {N3,A1}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {N3,N4}pk(A1)
A1 -> A2: {N4}pk(A2)

GOALS:
1. A2 authenticates A1 on N3
2. A1 authenticates A2 on N4
3. N3 secret between A1,A2
4. N4 secret between A1,A2

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0082

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4,N5;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk,sk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,pk(A1),inv(pk(A1)),a3,sk(a3),A2;
A2: A2,pk(A2),inv(pk(A2)),a3,sk(a3);
a3: a3,pk,sk,inv(sk(a3))
Actions:
A1 -> a3: A1,A2
a3 -> A1: {A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3))
A1 -> A2: {N4,A1}pk(A2)
A2 -> a3: A2,A1
a3 -> A2: {A1,pk(A1)}inv(sk(a3))
A2 -> A1: {N4,N5}pk(A1)
A1 -> A2: {N5}pk(A2)

GOALS:
1. A1 *->* A2: N4
2. A2 *->* A1: N5

A1 replies with {N5_1}pk(A2).\nSession 2: Attacker replays message {N4_1,A1}pk(A2) from Session 1 to A2. A1 has no way to verify that N5 is fresh or that A2 actually sent it in the current session. The protocol lacks mechanisms like signatures or nonce-challenge responses that would authenticate N5.",

A1 accepts N5_1 as fresh from A2, but A2 is unaware of this session and did not generate N5_1 for it."
        }

}

analysis.justification: The protocol does not provide secure authentication of N4 from A1 to A2. A2 receives N4 in message 3 ({N4,A1}pk(A2)), but this message lacks any authentication mechanism. An attacker can intercept and replay this message from a previous session, causing A2 to accept a stale N4 value without A1's current agreement. The subsequent certificate exchange with a3 occurs after N4 is already sent, providing no authentication for N4 itself. The signature from a3 only authenticates public keys, not the nonce N4.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 initiates with a3, gets A2's public key certificate, sends {N4_1,A1}pk(A2) to A2. A2 completes handshake with a3 and responds with {N4_1,N5_1}pk(A1). A1 replies with {N5_1}pk(A2).
Session 2: Attacker replays message {N4_1,A1}pk(A2) from Session 1 to A2. A2 processes it as fresh, completes certificate exchange with a3, and responds with {N4_1,N5_2}pk(A1). A2 believes it shares N4_1 with A1, but A1 is unaware of this session.

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide secure authentication of N5 from A2 to A1. A1 receives N5 in message 6 ({N5}pk(A2)), but this message can be replayed by an attacker. Since N5 is not bound to the session context or authenticated with A2's identity, an attacker can capture {N5}pk(A2) from a previous session and replay it to A1. A1 has no way to verify that N5 is fresh or that A2 actually sent it in the current session. The protocol lacks mechanisms like signatures or nonce-challenge responses that would authenticate N5.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Normal execution where A2 generates N5_1 and sends {N4_1,N5_1}pk(A1), then A1 replies with {N5_1}pk(A2).
Session 2: Attacker initiates new session with A1, receives {N4_2,A1}pk(A2) from A1, then replays {N5_1}pk(A2) from Session 1 to A1. A1 accepts N5_1 as fresh from A2, but A2 is unaware of this session and did not generate N5_1 for it.

analysis.justification: N4 is only ever sent inside {N4,A1}pk(A2) and later echoed inside {N4,N5}pk(A1). There is no authentication of the first ciphertext to A2 (no signature/MAC binding A1 to N4), so an attacker can start a run with A2 using its own chosen N4' while claiming identity A1. A2 will then accept N4' as coming from A1, violating secure-channel/authenticated-origin expectations for N4. Confidentiality of N4 may still hold, but the goal requires secure (confidential+authenticated) delivery, which fails.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->a3: A1,A2; a3->I: {A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)); I->A2: {N4',A1}pk(A2); A2 continues believing N4' is from A1.
Session 2: A1->a3: A1,A2; a3->A1: {A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)); A1->A2: {N4,A1}pk(A2) (honest run), but A2 may already have accepted N4' in Session 1 as the value for a run with 'A1'.

analysis.justification: A1 receives {N4,N5}pk(A1) and has no cryptographic evidence that it was created by A2 (no signature/MAC from A2). An attacker can choose any N5' and send {N4,N5'}pk(A1) to A1 after learning N4 from A1's first message (the attacker cannot decrypt it, but can run its own session to obtain a matching N4 it chose). A1 will then return {N5'}pk(A2), causing A1 to accept N5' as coming from A2 without authentication. Thus the secure-channel/authenticated-origin requirement for N5 fails.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->a3: A1,A2; a3->I: {A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)); I->A2: {N4',A1}pk(A2) (I chooses N4'); A2 (if it responds) would send {N4',N5_A2}pk(A1), but I instead forges next step.
Session 2: A1->a3: A1,A2; a3->A1: {A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)); A1->A2: {N4,A1}pk(A2); I->A1: {N4,N5'}pk(A1) (I chooses N5'); A1->A2: {N5'}pk(A2), so A1 treats N5' as from A2.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0084

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Agent a3;
Number empty,N6,N9,N7,N8;
HmacKey V4,V5;
SeqNumber S11,S10;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] can;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share can(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N9,N6
Actions:
A1 -> A2: V5,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4)
A2 -> A1: N7,S11,N8,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5))
A1 -> A2: {N9,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: N7,S11,N8,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)),hmac(N6,V5),{N9,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: S10,{hash(S10,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)))}inv(sk(a3))
A2 -> A1: S10,{hash(S10,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)))}inv(sk(a3))

GOALS:
1. can(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. A2 authenticates a3 on S10
3. A1 authenticates a3 on S10
4. A2 authenticates a3 on N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),S10
5. A1 authenticates a3 on N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),S10
6. N6 secret between A1,A2

Typically, Dolev-Yao assumes perfect cryptography, so no attack. But the goal might be violated if the attacker can learn N6 from other means. Since N6 is only used inside HMAC, and HMAC is keyed with N6, it's like a key. The attacker sees plaintext V5 and tag hmac(N6,V5). This is similar to a MAC, which doesn't reveal the key. So secrecy holds. Yet, confidence is moderate because if the HMAC is weak, attacks exist. But in symbolic model, we assume perfect crypto. So status should be 'no attack'. Re-evaluating: In symbolic analysis, if a term is used as a key in a function that outputs publicly, it might be derivable. But HMAC is considered a one-way function; the key is not derivable from output. So no attack.",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: A1 sends V5_1, hmac(can(A1,a3),V4_1). A2 replies with N7_1,S11_1,N8_1,hash(...), including hmac(N6,V5_1).\nSession 2: Similar with fresh V5_2, etc."
    }
  ]
}
```

analysis.justification: The term can(A1,a3) is sent encrypted under pk(a3) in message 3 from A1 to A2, and forwarded to a3 in message 4. Since a3 possesses inv(pk(a3)), it can decrypt and learn can(A1,a3). However, the goal requires secrecy between A1 and a3 only, and a3 is an intended recipient. The attack arises because A2 receives the encrypted term and forwards it; if A2 is compromised or the attacker intercepts, they cannot decrypt without inv(pk(a3)). But if the attacker compromises a3's private key, secrecy fails. Under Dolev-Yao, private keys are not compromised unless stated, but the protocol explicitly gives a3 inv(pk(a3)), so a3 legitimately learns it.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 sends V5,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4) to A2. A2 replies with N7,S11,N8,hash(...). A1 sends encrypted bundle to A2. A2 forwards to a3. a3 responds with S10,signature. A2 forwards to A1.
Session 2: Identical to Session 1 with fresh nonces.

analysis.justification: A2 receives S10 and a signature {hash(S10,hash(...))}inv(sk(a3)) from a3 in message 5. A2 can verify the signature using sk(a3) (public key). The signature covers S10 and the hash of previous messages, linking S10 to the protocol run. Since only a3 possesses inv(sk(a3)), the signature authenticates a3 as the sender of S10. A2 can check the hash includes values it knows (N7,S11,N8, etc.), ensuring freshness. No apparent replay attack because the hash includes nonces from A2 (N7,S11,N8).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 receives S10_1,{hash(S10_1,hash(...1))}inv(sk(a3)) from a3.
Session 2: A2 receives S10_2,{hash(S10_2,hash(...2))}inv(sk(a3)) from a3.

analysis.justification: A1 receives S10 and the signature from A2 in message 6, not directly from a3. The signature is {hash(S10,hash(...))}inv(sk(a3)), which A1 can verify using sk(a3). However, A1 does not directly authenticate a3 because the message is relayed by A2. An attacker could replay a previous signature from a3 to A1 via A2, but the hash includes N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5), which are specific to the run. If the attacker convinces A2 to initiate a run and then intercepts the signature, they might forward it to A1 out of context. But A1 expects the hash to match its own values.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 receives S10_1,{hash(S10_1,hash(...1))}inv(sk(a3)) from A2.
Session 2: A1 receives S10_2,{hash(S10_2,hash(...2))}inv(sk(a3)) from A2.

analysis.justification: A2 receives from a3 in message 5 a signature on hash(S10,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5))). This signature authenticates a3 on the hash of these values, but not individually on each value. However, since the hash is computed over all these values, and A2 knows them (except N9 and hmac(can(...),V4) which it learns from A1's messages), it can verify that a3 has seen and agrees to them. The signature indirectly authenticates a3 on the tuple. A2 must trust that a3 actually saw the values; a3 receives them in message 4. So authentication holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3 sends signature covering hash(S10_1,hash(N9_1,N7_1,S11_1,N8_1,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4_1),hmac(N6,V5_1))) to A2.
Session 2: Similar with fresh values.

analysis.justification: Similar to goal 4, but A1 receives the signature from A2. A1 can verify the signature using sk(a3). The signature covers the hash of these values, so A1 can check that a3 agrees on them. A1 knows all values: N9, N7,S11,N8 from message 2, hmac(can(...),V4) it computed, hmac(N6,V5) from message 2, and S10 from message 6. Thus, verification succeeds. However, A1 does not directly receive from a3, but the signature is cryptographically binding.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 receives signature covering hash(S10_1,hash(N9_1,N7_1,S11_1,N8_1,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4_1),hmac(N6,V5_1))) from A2.
Session 2: Similar.

analysis.justification: N6 is a number agreed between A1 and A2 (see Knowledge: A1,A2 agree N9,N6). It is used in hmac(N6,V5) in messages 2,4,5,6. The value hmac(N6,V5) is sent in clear in message 2 from A2 to A1, and later in message 4 from A2 to a3. An attacker observing these messages sees hmac(N6,V5). However, without knowing N6, they cannot compute the HMAC, but they might perform an offline attack if V5 is known. V5 is sent in clear in message 1 from A1 to A2.

Session 2: Similar with fresh V5_2, etc.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0085

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Agent a3;
Number empty,N6,N9,N7,N8;
HmacKey V4,V5;
SeqNumber S11,S10;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] can;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share can(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N9,N6
Actions:
A1 -> A2: V5,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4)
A2 -> A1: N7,S11,N8,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5))
A1 -> A2: {N9,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: N7,S11,N8,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)),hmac(N6,V5),{N9,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: S10,{A1,A2,hash(S10,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)))}inv(sk(a3))
A2 -> A1: S10,{A1,A2,hash(S10,hash(N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5)))}inv(sk(a3))

GOALS:
1. can(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. A2 authenticates a3 on S10
3. A1 authenticates a3 on S10
4. A2 authenticates a3 on N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),S10
5. A1 authenticates a3 on N9,N7,S11,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),S10
6. N6 secret between A1,A2

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0086

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1;
Agent A2,a3;
Number N6,N11,N7,N9,N10,N8;
SeqNumber S13,S12;
HmacKey V4,V5;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] can;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share can(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N11,N6
Actions:
A1 -> A2: V5,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4)
A2 -> A1: N7,S13,N8,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),hash(N9),hash(N10),{hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10))}inv(sk(A2))
A1 -> A2: {N11,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: N7,S13,N8,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),hash(N9),hash(N10),hmac(N6,V5),{N11,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3),{hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),{N11,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)}inv(sk(A2))
a3 -> A2: S12,{hash(S12,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)))}inv(sk(a3))
A2 -> A1: S12,{hash(S12,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)))}inv(sk(a3)),N9,N10

GOALS:
1. can(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. A1 authenticates A2 on N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)
3. a3 weakly authenticates A2 on N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)
4. A1 authenticates a3 on S12
5. A1 authenticates a3 on N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10),S12
6. N6 secret between A1,A2

analysis.justification: The term can(A1,a3) is sent by A1 inside {N11,hash(...),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3). Since pk(a3) is public, the attacker can encrypt any chosen plaintext under pk(a3) and later obtain can(A1,a3) by compromising a3 or by acting as a3 if the model allows the attacker to control a3’s private key in a separate session. Moreover, can(A1,a3) is also used as an HMAC key in hmac(can(A1,a3),V4); if the attacker learns V4 (it is not protected anywhere), this enables offline guessing/derivation in symbolic models where keys are not guessable but may be exposed via other means. Overall, secrecy of can(A1,a3) is not preserved under standard Dolev-Yao with possible role compromise across sessions.

Session 2: I runs as a3 (or compromises inv(pk(a3))) and decrypts the recorded ciphertext to obtain can(A1,a3).

analysis.justification: A1’s evidence about A2 is the signed hash {hash(...)}inv(sk(A2)) received in A2 -> A1 message 2. However, the signed material is only a hash and does not bind the peer identity A1 nor a session-unique challenge from A1; an attacker can replay an old valid A2 signature and associated fields to A1. Because A1’s first message does not include a nonce from A1 that is later signed by A2, A1 cannot ensure freshness/injectivity and can be convinced of agreement on stale values. Thus authentication of A2 on the listed tuple fails due to replay.

Session 2: Attacker replays the recorded A2 -> A1 message to A1; A1 accepts it as coming from A2 and believes the tuple is agreed, without a matching fresh run by A2.

analysis.justification: a3 receives from A2 a message containing {hash(...),{N11,hash(...),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)}inv(sk(A2)), which is a signature verifiable with sk(A2). This signature binds the hash value and the exact ciphertext intended for a3, so an attacker cannot forge it without inv(sk(A2)). Even if the attacker replays an old signed package, weak authentication (non-injective) still holds because it only requires that some run of A2 produced that signed data. Therefore, under Dolev-Yao without A2 key compromise, a3 can weakly authenticate A2 on the tuple embedded in the signed hash.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> a3: ... , {hash(...),{N11,hash(...),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)}inv(sk(A2)); a3 verifies signature with sk(A2) and accepts.
Session 2: Attacker replays the same A2 -> a3 message; a3 again verifies it as originating from A2 (still consistent with weak authentication).

analysis.justification: A1 receives S12 and a3’s signature {hash(S12,hash(...))}inv(sk(a3)) only via A2 in the final message. A1 has no direct channel to a3 and no guarantee that S12 is fresh for A1; an attacker can replay an old (S12, signature) pair previously produced by a3 and forward it through A2 (or impersonate A2 on the network). Since the signature does not include any A1-chosen nonce or session identifier unique to A1’s current run, A1 can accept S12 as authenticated from a3 even when a3 did not participate in this run. Hence authentication of a3 on S12 fails due to replay.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest a3 -> A2: S12,{hash(S12,hash(...))}inv(sk(a3)); attacker records this pair.
Session 2: Attacker sends to A1 (posing as A2) the recorded S12 and signature; A1 verifies with sk(a3) and accepts S12 without a fresh a3 run.

analysis.justification: A1’s only a3-authenticating evidence is the signature {hash(S12,hash(...))}inv(sk(a3)), where hash(...) corresponds to values that A1 previously received (purportedly) from A2. An attacker can mix-and-match by replaying an old a3 signature over (S12, old hash(...)) while separately replaying or substituting the A2 message that provides the same old hash(...). Because A1 does not contribute a fresh nonce that is included in a3’s signature, A1 cannot ensure that a3’s signature is linked to A1’s current run. Therefore A1 can be led to accept the full tuple with S12 without a3 participating in the current session.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker records A2 -> A1 message 2 containing hash(...old...) and later records a3 -> A2 message containing S12_old,{hash(S12_old,hash(...old...))}inv(sk(a3)).
Session 2: Attacker replays A2 -> A1 message 2 (old) and then replays to A1 (as A2) S12_old and the a3 signature; A1 accepts the combined tuple as authenticated from a3.

analysis.justification: N6 is used as an HMAC key in hmac(N6,V5) and is never sent in clear, but the protocol does not state that N6 is initially unknown to the attacker; it is only said that A1 and A2 agree N11,N6. If the attacker can choose V5 (it is sent by A1 without protection and could be replayed/substituted), then hmac(N6,V5) becomes an oracle value that can be replayed and correlated across sessions, violating secrecy in the sense of distinguishability. Additionally, if A2 is compromised in a second session (inv(pk(A2)) etc.), N6 can be learned from A2’s initial knowledge, breaking the “between A1,A2” secrecy claim. Under standard symbolic analysis with possible agent compromise across sessions, the goal does not hold.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: V5,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4); A2 later sends hmac(N6,V5) inside its message; attacker records hmac(N6,V5).
Session 2: Attacker compromises/controls A2 (learns its long-term secrets/knowledge including N6) and thus learns N6, violating secrecy between only A1 and A2.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0087

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1;
Agent A2,a3;
Number N6,N11,N7,N9,N10,N8;
SeqNumber S13,S12;
HmacKey V4,V5;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] can;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share can(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N11,N6
Actions:
A1 -> A2: V5,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4)
A2 -> A1: N7,S13,N8,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),hash(N9),hash(N10),{hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10))}inv(sk(A2))
A1 -> A2: {N11,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: N7,S13,N8,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),hash(N9),hash(N10),hmac(N6,V5),{N11,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3),{a3,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),{N11,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)),can(A1,a3),V4}pk(a3)}inv(sk(A2))
a3 -> A2: S12,{A1,A2,hash(S12,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)))}inv(sk(a3))
A2 -> A1: S12,{A1,A2,hash(S12,hash(N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)))}inv(sk(a3)),N9,N10

GOALS:
1. can(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. A1 authenticates A2 on N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)
3. a3 weakly authenticates A2 on N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10)
4. A2 authenticates a3 on S12
5. A1 authenticates a3 on S12
6. A2 authenticates a3 on N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10),S12
7. A1 authenticates a3 on N11,N7,S13,N8,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N6,V5),hash(N9),hash(N10),S12
8. N6 secret between A1,A2
