prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0088

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N14,N6,empty,N7,N13,N8,N10,N11,N12,N9;
HmacKey V4,V5;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] can;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A1)),inv(hk(A1)),inv(sk(A1)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share can(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N13,N7
Actions:
A1 -> A2: V5,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4)
A2 -> A1: N8,N14,N9,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),hash(N11),hash(N12),{hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12))}inv(sk(A2))
A1 -> A2: {N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3),{hash({N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3),hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)))}inv(sk(A1))
A2 -> a3: N8,N14,N9,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),hash(N11),hash(N12),hmac(N7,V5),{N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3),{hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),{N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3)}inv(sk(A2)),{hash({N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3),hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)))}inv(sk(A1))
a3 -> A2: N6,{hash(N6,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)))}inv(sk(a3))
A2 -> A1: N6,{hash(N6,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)))}inv(sk(a3)),N11,N12

GOALS:
1. can(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on can(A1,a3)
3. a3 weakly authenticates A2 on N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)
4. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)
5. A2 authenticates a3 on N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12),N6
6. A1 authenticates a3 on N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12),N6
7. A2 authenticates a3 on N6
8. A1 authenticates a3 on N6
9. N7 secret between A1,A2

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0089

PROTOCOL_BODY:

Number N14,N6,empty,N7,N13,N8,N10,N11,N12,N9;

Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A1)),inv(hk(A1)),inv(sk(A1)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,can(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share can(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N13,N7
Actions:
A1 -> A2: V5,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4)
A2 -> A1: N8,N14,N9,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),hash(N11),hash(N12),{hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12))}inv(sk(A2))
A1 -> A2: {N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3),{hash({N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3),hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)))}inv(sk(A1))
A2 -> a3: N8,N14,N9,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),hash(N11),hash(N12),hmac(N7,V5),{N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3),{hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),{N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3)}inv(sk(A2)),{hash({N13,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)),can(A1,a3),V4,N10}pk(a3),hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)))}inv(sk(A1))
a3 -> A2: N6,{A1,A2,hash(N6,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)))}inv(sk(a3))
A2 -> A1: N6,{A1,A2,hash(N6,hash(N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)))}inv(sk(a3)),N11,N12

GOALS:
1. can(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on can(A1,a3)
3. A1 authenticates A2 on N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)
4. a3 weakly authenticates A2 on N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)
5. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12)
6. A2 authenticates a3 on N6
7. A1 authenticates a3 on N13,N8,N14,N9,hmac(can(A1,a3),V4),hmac(N7,V5),hash(N11),hash(N12),N6
8. A1 authenticates a3 on N6
9. N7 secret between A1,A2

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0090

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N6,N4,N5;
Symmetric_key K7;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,shk(A2,a3);
a3: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3),shk(A2,a3);
A1,a3 share shk(A1,a3);
A2,a3 share shk(A2,a3);
where A1!=A2
Actions:
A1 -> A2: N6,A1,A2,{|N4,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A1,a3)
A2 -> a3: N6,A1,A2,{|N4,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A1,a3),{|N5,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A2,a3)
a3 -> A2: N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3),{|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3)
A2 -> A1: N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3)

GOALS:
1. K7 secret between A1,A2,a3
2. A2 authenticates a3 on K7
3. A1 authenticates a3 on K7

A2 forwards N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) to A1.\nSession 2: Identical to Session 1 with different nonces. A2 can decrypt {|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3) in both sessions, learning K7."

A2 forwards N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) to A1.\nSession 2: A2 replays the encrypted {|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) from Session 1 to A1 without contacting a3. A1 accepts it as valid since it's properly encrypted under shk(A1,a3)."

analysis.justification: The symmetric key K7 is encrypted under shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are only known to A1-a3 and A2-a3 pairs respectively. However, A2 receives both encrypted versions of K7 and forwards one to A1. Since A1 and A2 don't share a direct symmetric key, and A2 can decrypt the version encrypted under shk(A2,a3), A2 learns K7. The protocol doesn't establish K7 as a secret shared among all three parties simultaneously, but rather as pairwise secrets between a3 and each of A1 and A2.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 sends N6,A1,A2,{|N4,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A1,a3) to A2. A2 forwards to a3 with added {|N5,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A2,a3). a3 responds with N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3),{|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3). A2 forwards N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) to A1.
Session 2: Identical to Session 1 with different nonces. A2 can decrypt {|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3) in both sessions, learning K7.

analysis.justification: A2 receives K7 encrypted under shk(A2,a3), a key only shared between A2 and a3. The encryption includes N5, which A2 previously sent to a3 encrypted under the same key. This provides freshness and origin authentication. An attacker cannot forge this message without knowing shk(A2,a3), and replay is prevented by the inclusion of N5.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 sends N6,A1,A2,{|N4,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A1,a3),{|N5,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A2,a3) to a3. a3 responds with N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3),{|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3). A2 verifies the response contains N5.
Session 2: Same pattern with different nonces. Each response from a3 is uniquely bound to A2's request through N5.

analysis.justification: A1 receives K7 encrypted under shk(A1,a3), but this message comes from A2, not directly from a3. A2 could replay an old message from a3 or forward a message from a different session. The protocol doesn't include sufficient freshness elements in the final message to A1 to guarantee it originated from a3 in the current session. A1 cannot distinguish between a genuine response from a3 forwarded by A2 and a replayed message.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 sends N6,A1,A2,{|N4,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A1,a3) to A2. a3 responds to A2 with N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3),{|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3). A2 forwards N6,{|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) to A1.
Session 2: A2 replays the encrypted {|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) from Session 1 to A1 without contacting a3. A1 accepts it as valid since it's properly encrypted under shk(A1,a3).

analysis.justification: K7 is only ever transmitted inside {|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) and {|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3). Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker cannot decrypt these without shk(A1,a3) or shk(A2,a3), which are not sent on the network. Even if the attacker replays or reorders messages, they only see ciphertexts and cannot derive K7. Thus confidentiality of K7 against an external network attacker holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1->A2: N6,A1,A2,C1 where C1={|N4,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A1,a3); A2->a3: N6,A1,A2,C1,C2 where C2={|N5,N6,A1,A2|}shk(A2,a3); a3->A2: N6,C3,C4 where C3={|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3), C4={|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3); A2->A1: N6,C3.
Session 2: Attacker replays a3->A2 message from Session 1 to A2 and A2->A1 forwards; attacker still cannot decrypt C3/C4 and cannot obtain K7.

analysis.justification: A2 accepts K7 upon receiving {|N5,K7|}shk(A2,a3), but this message is not bound to the current run’s N6 (nor to A2’s fresh contribution) and can be replayed. An attacker can record a prior a3->A2 response and later replay it to A2 in a different session, causing A2 to accept an old K7 while believing it is from a3 for the new run. This violates injective agreement and also non-injective agreement if freshness of K7 for the run is intended.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run. A1->A2: N6_1,A1,A2,C1_1; A2->a3: N6_1,A1,A2,C1_1,C2_1; a3->A2: N6_1,C3_1={|N4_1,K7_1|}shk(A1,a3), C4_1={|N5_1,K7_1|}shk(A2,a3). Attacker records C4_1.
Session 2: New run starts with fresh N6_2. A1->A2: N6_2,A1,A2,C1_2; attacker blocks A2->a3 and instead sends to A2: N6_2, (any C3), C4_1. A2 decrypts C4_1 with shk(A2,a3) and accepts K7_1 as if from a3 in Session 2.

analysis.justification: A1 accepts K7 from the message {|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) forwarded by A2, but this ciphertext is not tied to the session identifier N6 in the final step. An attacker can replay an old {|N4,K7|}shk(A1,a3) from a previous run into a new run, and A1 will accept the old K7 while believing it corresponds to the current execution involving a3. Therefore agreement on K7 with a3 is vulnerable to replay across sessions.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run. a3 creates K7_1 and sends to A2: N6_1,C3_1={|N4_1,K7_1|}shk(A1,a3),C4_1; A2 forwards to A1: N6_1,C3_1. Attacker records C3_1.
Session 2: A1 initiates with fresh N6_2: A1->A2: N6_2,A1,A2,C1_2. Attacker blocks A2->A1 legitimate response and instead sends to A1: N6_2,C3_1. A1 decrypts C3_1 using shk(A1,a3) and accepts K7_1 as if from a3 for Session 2.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0091

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Number N3;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,inv(pk(A1));
A2: A2,pk
Actions:
A1 -> A2: A1
A2 -> A1: {N3}pk(A1)

GOALS:
1. A2 ->* A1: N3

analysis.justification: The protocol uses asymmetric encryption with A1's public key to protect N3. Since only A1 possesses the corresponding private key inv(pk(A1)), the Dolev-Yao attacker cannot decrypt {N3}pk(A1) without it. The channel notation '->*' requires confidentiality, which is satisfied as the encryption prevents the attacker from learning N3 during transmission. No replay or man-in-the-middle attacks compromise this confidentiality property.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 sends A1 to A2. A2 generates N3_1, encrypts it as {N3_1}pk(A1), and sends to A1. A1 decrypts to obtain N3_1.
Session 2: A1 sends A1 to A2. A2 generates N3_2, encrypts it as {N3_2}pk(A1), and sends to A1. A1 decrypts to obtain N3_2.

analysis.justification: N3 is only sent as {N3}pk(A1), so under Dolev-Yao the attacker cannot decrypt it without inv(pk(A1)). A1 is the only party stated to know inv(pk(A1)), and A2 does not reveal N3 elsewhere. Even if the attacker replays or redirects the ciphertext, confidentiality of N3 is preserved because the ciphertext remains under pk(A1). The only caveat is that pk is assumed correctly bound to A1 (no key-substitution modeled here).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->A2: A1 (impersonating A1). A2->I: {N3_1}pk(A1). I cannot derive N3_1.
Session 2: A1->I(A2): A1. I(A2)->A1: {N3_2}pk(A1) (chosen by attacker-as-A2). I still cannot derive N3_2 from the ciphertext.

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0092

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,a2;
Number N3;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,inv(pk(A1));
a2: a2,pk
Actions:
A1 -> a2: A1
a2 -> A1: {N3}pk(A1)

GOALS:
1. a2 ->* A1: N3

analysis.justification: The goal requires confidential transmission of N3 from a2 to A1, but the protocol uses asymmetric encryption without authentication. An attacker can intercept the encrypted message {N3}pk(A1) and replay it to A1 in a different session. Since A1 can decrypt it with inv(pk(A1)), they will accept N3, but the attacker learns N3 through the replay, breaking confidentiality. The channel notation A1 -> a2: A1 shows no confidentiality protection for the initial message, and the response lacks freshness guarantees.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> a2: A1; a2 -> A1: {N3_1}pk(A1)
Session 2: Attacker replays {N3_1}pk(A1) to A1 as if from a2, and A1 decrypts to obtain N3_1

analysis.justification: The protocol does not authenticate a2 to A1: A1 first sends only its identity, so an attacker can trigger a2 to encrypt N3 to A1 without being a2. The confidentiality of N3 against the network holds (it is encrypted under pk(A1)), but the stated goal is a confidential-channel claim from a2 to A1, which also implies the message originates from a2. Since A1 cannot distinguish whether {N3}pk(A1) came from a2 or an attacker relaying/initiating, the goal is violated by an impersonation/unknown-origin attack.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I -> a2: A1 ; a2 -> A1: {N3_1}pk(A1) (A1 receives N3_1 but peer is actually I, not a2).
Session 2: A1 -> a2: A1 ; I intercepts a2 -> A1: {N3_2}pk(A1) and forwards it to A1 (A1 cannot authenticate that a2 sent it).

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0093

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Agent a3;
Number N5,N6,N7,N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] ccn;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
HmacKey Hx3a3ccnA1a3;
SeqNumber SQNx9;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,ccn(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,ccn(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share ccn(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N6,N5
Actions:
A1 -> A2: hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hash(N5)
A2 -> A1: N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hash(N5))
A1 -> A2: {N6,N7,ccn(A1,a3),hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hash(N5))}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: {N6,N7,ccn(A1,a3),hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hash(N5))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: empty
A2 -> a3: {N6,N7,hash(N5),hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hash(N5))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: {N4,N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hash(N5)),SQNx9,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))
A2 -> A1: {N4,N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hash(N5)),SQNx9,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))

GOALS:
1. ccn(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. A2 authenticates a3 on N4
3. A1 authenticates a3 on N4
4. A2 authenticates a3 on hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hash(N5)),N4
5. A1 authenticates a3 on hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hash(N5)),N4
6. N5 secret between A1,A2

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0094

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1;
Agent A2,a3;
Number N5,N6,N7,N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] ccn;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
HmacKey Hx3A2N5;
HmacKey Hx3a3ccnA1a3;
SeqNumber SQNx4;
SeqNumber SQNx8;
SeqNumber SQNx9;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,ccn(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,ccn(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share ccn(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N6,N5
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5),{Hx3A2N5}hk(A2)}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {A1,SQNx4,N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))}inv(sk(A2))
A1 -> A2: {N6,N7,ccn(A1,a3),hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: {N6,N7,ccn(A1,a3),hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: empty
A2 -> a3: {{a3,SQNx8,N6,hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5),{Hx3A2N5}hk(A2),N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))}inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: {{N4,N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5)),SQNx9,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {N4,N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5)),SQNx9,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))

GOALS:
1. ccn(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3

analysis.justification: A1 receives the hash value inside A2’s signature: {A1,SQNx4,N7,hash(...)}inv(sk(A2)). The attacker cannot forge A2’s signature, so any accepted hash(...) must have been signed by A2. Since the hash binds N7 and the two HMAC terms, A1 can associate it with its run provided it checks N7 consistency.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2->A1: {A1,SQNx4,N7,hash(...)}inv(sk(A2)); A1 verifies signature and accepts A2 on hash(...).
Session 2: Attacker replays Session 1 signature; if A1 checks N7 freshness/match, replay fails; otherwise it could accept an old hash, but still it authenticates A2 as signer (non-injective).

analysis.justification: a3 receives A2’s signature inside an encryption to pk(a3): {{a3,SQNx8,N6,hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5),{Hx3A2N5}hk(A2),N7,hash(...)}inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3). Only A2 can produce inv(sk(A2)), so a3’s acceptance of hash(...) implies A2 participated at least once (weak authentication). Replay could allow non-injective agreement, but the goal is explicitly weak.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2->a3: {{...,hash(...)}inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3); a3 decrypts and verifies A2 signature.
Session 2: Attacker replays the same ciphertext to a3; a3 may accept again (non-injective), but still the signer is A2, satisfying weak authentication.

analysis.justification: A2 obtains both N4 and hash(...) in a3’s signed message encrypted to A2: {{N4,N7,hash(...),SQNx9,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2). The attacker cannot forge a3’s signature, so A2’s acceptance of the pair (hash(...),N4) implies a3 generated it. Potential replay exists, but the inclusion of N7 and SQNx9 provides run linkage if checked.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3->A2: {{N4,N7,hash(...),SQNx9,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2); A2 verifies and accepts a3 on (hash(...),N4).
Session 2: Attacker replays Session 1 message; with standard freshness checks on N7/SQNx9, A2 rejects; otherwise it is non-injective but still from a3.

analysis.justification: A1 learns (N4,hash(...)) only via A2 forwarding a3’s signature over an insecure channel, without direct secure delivery from a3. An attacker can replay an old a3-signed tuple (N4_old,hash_old,...) to A1 in a new session, and A1 may accept it as evidence of a3’s current participation. Therefore agreement of A1 with a3 on the pair (hash(...),N4) is vulnerable to replay/misbinding across sessions.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest completion; attacker records A2->A1: {N4_old,N7_old,hash_old,SQNx9_old,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3)).
Session 2: Attacker induces A1 to expect final data, then replays the recorded signed message; A1 accepts (hash_old,N4_old) as from a3 without a fresh matching a3 run.

analysis.justification: N5 is used as the key in hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5), and Hx3A2N5 is sent encrypted under hk(A2) in the first message. Since A2 knows inv(hk(A2)), A2 can recover Hx3A2N5, and then the attacker (who sees hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5) on the network) can mount an offline guessing attack on N5 if N5 is a low-entropy Number. In symbolic models, such key-guessing is typically represented as an attack when a secret is used as an HMAC key over known data.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker observes hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5) and {Hx3A2N5}hk(A2) inside { ... }pk(A2); after A2 decrypts, Hx3A2N5 may be exposed to attacker via compromise/assumption of guessability, enabling offline test of N5.
Session 2: Same structure repeats, giving attacker multiple HMAC samples to validate guesses of N5.

analysis.justification: N6 is only transmitted inside messages encrypted to pk(a3) (from A1 and from A2) and inside A2’s signature encrypted to pk(a3), and never appears in plaintext. Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker cannot decrypt pk(a3) ciphertexts without inv(pk(a3)), so N6 is not derivable from the network. The intended recipients A1/A2/a3 can know N6 through their roles, but the attacker cannot.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1->A2: {...}pk(A2); A1->A2 and A2->a3: {N6,...}pk(a3); attacker cannot decrypt, so cannot learn N6.
Session 2: Even with replay/interleaving, N6 remains only under pk(a3) encryption; attacker still cannot derive it.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0095

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N5,N6,N7,N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] ccn;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
HmacKey Hx3A2N5;
HmacKey Hx3a3ccnA1a3;
SeqNumber SQNx3;
SeqNumber SQNx4;
SeqNumber SQNx8;
SeqNumber SQNx9;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A1)),inv(hk(A1)),inv(sk(A1)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,ccn(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,ccn(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share ccn(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N6,N5
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {{hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5),{Hx3A2N5}hk(A2),SQNx3,A2}inv(sk(A1))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {{A1,SQNx4,N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))}inv(sk(A2))}pk(A1)
A1 -> A2: {{N6,N7,ccn(A1,a3),hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5)),a3}inv(sk(A1))}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: {{N6,N7,ccn(A1,a3),hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),{Hx3a3ccnA1a3}hk(a3),hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5)),a3}inv(sk(A1))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: empty
A2 -> a3: {{a3,SQNx8,N6,N7,hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5),{Hx3A2N5}hk(A2),hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))}inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: {{N4,N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5)),SQNx9,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {{N4,N7,hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5)),SQNx9,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A1)

GOALS:
1. ccn(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on ccn(A1,a3)
3. A2 authenticates a3 on N4
4. A1 authenticates a3 on N4
5. A1 authenticates A2 on hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))
6. a3 weakly authenticates A2 on hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))
7. A2 authenticates a3 on hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5)),N4
8. A1 authenticates a3 on hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5)),N4
9. A2 authenticates A1 on hash(N6,N7,hmac(ccn(A1,a3),Hx3a3ccnA1a3),hmac(N5,Hx3A2N5))
10. N5 secret between A1,A2
11. N4 secret between A1,A2,a3
12. N6 secret between A1,A2,a3

Since N5 is not used in any encrypted form directly, and only its HMAC is sent, the attacker cannot derive N5 from the HMAC without the key, but if Hx3A2N5 is compromised, N5 could be exposed. Given the lack of explicit encryption of N5, and the HMAC key potentially being derivable, this is a weakness.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 and A2 use N5 in HMAC.
Session 2: Attacker compromises Hx3A2N5 and learns N5 from observed HMAC.

analysis.justification: N4 is sent encrypted under pk(A2) in message 7 and under pk(A1) in message 8. Since encryption is asymmetric and public keys are known, anyone can encrypt to A1 or A2, but only A1 and A2 can decrypt. However, if the attacker compromises A1 or A2, N4 is exposed. Under Dolev-Yao without compromise, secrecy holds, but the goal includes a3 as a party, and a3 knows N4 as it generates it. The attack arises if A1 or A2 is malicious and leaks N4 to the attacker.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3 generates N4 and sends to A1 and A2.
Session 2: Attacker compromises A2 and learns N4.

analysis.justification: N6 is sent in cleartext within message 3 from A1 to A2, encrypted under pk(A2), and again in message 4 to a3. Since A2 and a3 are legitimate recipients, but if the attacker impersonates A2 or a3, N6 can be obtained. Additionally, N6 is part of the hash sent in multiple messages, but the hash does not reveal N6 directly. However, the explicit transmission to A2 and a3 means it is not secret from them, and if either is compromised, N6 is exposed.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 sends N6 to A2 in message 3.
Session 2: Attacker impersonates A2 to A1 and receives N6.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0096

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Number N3;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,pk;
A2: inv(pk(A2))
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {N3}pk(A2)

GOALS:
1. A1 ->* A2: N3
2. N3 secret between A1,A2

analysis.justification: N3 is sent only as {N3}pk(A2) on an insecure channel, but confidentiality relies on A2 being the only party able to decrypt with inv(pk(A2)). Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker can intercept and replay the ciphertext but cannot derive N3 without inv(pk(A2)). No other message reveals N3 or a related value that would allow deduction. analysis.justification: The only occurrence of N3 on the network is inside public-key encryption for A2, so the attacker cannot obtain N3 as a term. Since the attacker’s view of executions differs only by ciphertexts under pk(A2), and Dolev-Yao treats encryption as opaque without the private key, the attacker cannot distinguish which N3 was used beyond seeing an uninterpretable ciphertext. Replays do not help because they do not reveal the plaintext. Therefore the secrecy goal (indistinguishability) is satisfied.

Session 2: A1 chooses N3=nb; sends {nb}pk(A2); attacker observes ciphertext C2 only; cannot distinguish na vs nb without inv(pk(A2)).

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0099

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Number N3,N4;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2;
A2: A1,A2
Actions:
A1 -> A2: N3,N4

GOALS:
1. A1 -> A2: N3,N4
2. A1 ->* A2: N3,N4
3. A1 ->* A2: N3
4. A2 authenticates A1 on N3,N4

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0100

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N10,N8,N9,N7,N5,N6,N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] pan;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A1)),inv(hk(A1)),inv(sk(A1)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,pan(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,pan(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share pan(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N10,N9
Actions:
A1 -> A2: N7,N5
A2 -> A1: {N7,N8,N5,N6}inv(sk(A2))
A1 -> A2: {hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6)}inv(sk(A1)),{N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6),pan(A1,a3)}pk(a3),N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6,hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3))
A2 -> a3: {{N7,N8,hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6),hash(N9,N10),{hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6)}inv(sk(A1)),{N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6),pan(A1,a3)}pk(a3)}inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: {{A2,N7,N8,N10,N4}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4}inv(sk(A2))

GOALS:
1. pan(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on pan(A1,a3)
3. A2 authenticates a3 on N4
4. A1 authenticates A2 on N4
5. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on N10,N7,N8,hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6)
6. A1 authenticates A2 on N10,N7,N8,hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6)
7. A2 authenticates A1 on N10,N7,N8,hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6)
8. N9 secret between A1,A2
9. N10 secret between A1,A2,a3

analysis.goal: a3 weakly authenticates A1 on pan(A1,a3)
analysis.justification: a3 receives, under pk(a3), a bundle that contains A1's signature over hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan),pan) and hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6), and also contains pan itself. To make a3 accept pan as coming from A1, an attacker would need to forge A1's signature or obtain inv(sk(A1)), which is not available. Even though pan is leaked elsewhere, a3's acceptance is tied to A1's signature, so weak authentication of A1 on pan holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: ...; A2 -> a3: { ... {SigA1(...pan...), EncToA3(...pan...)} signed by A2 }pk(a3); a3 verifies SigA1 and binds pan to A1.
Session 2: Attacker replays old A1-signed material to a3; a3 may accept again (non-injective allowed), but still attributes pan to A1, not attacker.

analysis.justification: A2 receives message 5 as {{A2,N7,N8,N10,N4}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2), i.e., encrypted to A2 and signed by a3. Only a3 can produce a valid signature under inv(sk(a3)), so if A2 accepts N4 from that message, it is authenticated as originating from a3. Replay is possible but does not break basic (non-injective) authentication on N4.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3 -> A2: {{A2,N7,N8,N10,N4}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2); A2 decrypts and verifies signature, accepting N4 from a3.
Session 2: Attacker replays the same ciphertext to A2; A2 may accept again, but still authenticates a3 as the source of N4.

analysis.justification: A1 receives N4 only in message 6: {A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4}inv(sk(A2)), which is signed by A2. An attacker cannot forge A2's signature without inv(sk(A2)), so any accepted N4 must have been signed by A2. While N4 is originally generated by a3, the goal is about A2's authentication to A1, which is satisfied by A2's signature.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: {A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4}inv(sk(A2)); A1 verifies signature and accepts N4 as from A2.
Session 2: Attacker replays the signed message 6; A1 may accept again, but still attributes N4 to A2 (non-injective).

analysis.justification: a3's evidence for these items is A1's signature in message 4 (forwarded by A2) over exactly the two hashes that commit to N7,N8,N10 and to the second hash value. To make a3 accept those values as from A1, the attacker must present a valid A1 signature, which is unforgeable in the model. Even if the attacker replays an old signed tuple, weak (non-injective) authentication still holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> a3: { ... {hash1,hash2}inv(sk(A1)) ... }pk(a3); a3 verifies SigA1 and accepts the committed N7,N8,N10 and hash2.
Session 2: Attacker replays the same A1-signed pair to a3; a3 may accept again, but still authenticates A1 on the same data (weakly).

analysis.justification: A1 never receives from A2 any message that contains the two specified hash values (or enough data to bind them) under A2's signature. The only A2-signed messages to A1 are message 2 (covers N7,N8,N5,N6) and message 6 (covers A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4), neither of which includes hash(N9,N10) or the required hash terms. Thus A1 cannot establish agreement with A2 on the stated data; an attacker can drive A1 to complete without A2 ever having sent those specific values to A1.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker relays A1 -> A2: N7,N5; relays A2 -> A1: {N7,N8,N5,N6}SigA2; A1 sends message 3; later attacker delivers to A1 a valid {A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4}SigA2 from some run; A1 completes without ever receiving hash1/hash2 from A2.
Session 2: A2 runs separately with attacker; attacker obtains another message 6 signature and reuses it to make A1 believe A2 agreed on the larger tuple, though A2 never sent that tuple to A1.

analysis.justification: A2 does not receive N10 or hash(N9,N10) from A1 in any signed form; message 3 includes hash(N9,N10) and N10 only inside the encryption to a3 and inside a plaintext hash value, but not inside A1's signature in a way A2 can verify as binding N10. The A1 signature sent to A2 in message 3 covers only the two hash values, and A2 cannot extract N10 from those hashes. Therefore A2 cannot authenticate A1 on the tuple that explicitly includes N10; an attacker can replay A1's signed hashes from another context without A2 having evidence of the concrete N10 value.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: ... {hash1,hash2}SigA1 , ... ; A2 verifies SigA1 and proceeds, but has no signed/explicit N10 value from A1.
Session 2: Attacker replays {hash1,hash2}SigA1 from Session 1 into a new run with A2 while choosing a different N10 context; A2 still accepts SigA1 but cannot ensure agreement on the explicit N10.

analysis.justification: N9 never appears in plaintext on the network; only hash(N9,N10) is transmitted. Under Dolev-Yao, hashes are one-way and do not reveal N9 without additional equations, so the attacker cannot derive N9 from hash(N9,N10). Provided N9 is not initially known to the attacker (not listed in attacker knowledge), confidentiality of N9 holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker observes hash(N9,N10) in plaintext fields but cannot invert hash to obtain N9.
Session 2: Even with multiple observed hash(N9,N10) values across runs, attacker still cannot derive N9 symbolically.

analysis.justification: N10 is sent in the clear from A1 to A2 in message 3 as a standalone field. Since the channel is insecure, the attacker learns N10 by eavesdropping. Therefore N10 cannot be secret between A1, A2, and a3.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: ... N10 ... (attacker reads N10).
Session 2: Attacker uses known N10 to correlate or craft further messages; secrecy already violated from Session 1.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0101

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1;
Agent A2,a3;
Number N10,N8,N9,N7,N5,N6,N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] pan;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,pan(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,pan(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share pan(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N10,N9
Actions:
A1 -> A2: N7,N5
A2 -> A1: {N7,N8,N5,N6}inv(sk(A2))
A1 -> A2: hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6),{N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6),pan(A1,a3)}pk(a3),N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6,hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3))
A2 -> a3: {{N7,N8,hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6),hash(N9,N10),hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6),{N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6),pan(A1,a3)}pk(a3)}inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: {{A2,N7,N8,N10,N4}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4}inv(sk(A2))

GOALS:
1. pan(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. A2 authenticates a3 on N4
3. A1 authenticates A2 on N4
4. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on N10,N7,N8,hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6)
5. A1 authenticates A2 on N10,N7,N8,hash(N7,N8,hash(N9,N10),N10,A2,hash(N8,pan(A1,a3)),pan(A1,a3)),hash(N7,N8,N5,hash(N9,N10),N6)
6. N9 secret between A1,A2

analysis.justification: pan(A1,a3) is sent in the clear from A1 to A2 in message 3 (it appears as a standalone field and also inside hashes). Since A1->A2 is on an insecure channel, the attacker learns pan(A1,a3) by eavesdropping. This violates secrecy between A1 and a3 regardless of any later encryption to a3. No cryptographic break is needed; it is direct disclosure.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: ... , pan(A1,a3) , ... (attacker eavesdrops and learns pan(A1,a3)).
Session 2: Attacker replays/observes any run; pan(A1,a3) already known, so secrecy remains broken.

analysis.justification: A2 receives N4 only in message 5 as {{A2,N7,N8,N10,N4}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2). Only a3 can produce the inner signature with inv(sk(a3)), and only A2 can decrypt the outer layer with inv(pk(A2)). The signed tuple binds N4 to A2 and to the session values N7,N8,N10, preventing an attacker from substituting N4 without forging a3's signature. Replay across sessions would require matching N7,N8,N10, which are fresh per run from A1/A2.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> a3: ... ; a3 -> A2: {{A2,N7,N8,N10,N4}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) (A2 verifies signature as from a3).
Session 2: Attacker replays old message 5 to A2, but A2's expected N7,N8,N10 differ, so it is rejected as not matching the current run.

analysis.justification: A1 receives N4 only in message 6: {A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4}inv(sk(A2)). Only A2 can generate this signature, so an attacker cannot make A1 accept a chosen N4 without forging inv(sk(A2)). The inclusion of N7,N8,N5 and hash(N10) ties the message to A1's earlier values and the run context, limiting replay to runs with identical parameters. Under Dolev-Yao, this provides authentication of A2 on N4.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: ... ; A2 -> A1: {A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4}inv(sk(A2)) (A1 verifies as from A2).
Session 2: Attacker replays Session 1's message 6 to A1 in a new run with different N7/N8/N5/hash(N10); A1 detects mismatch and does not accept it for the new run.

analysis.justification: a3 never receives any message authenticated by A1 (no A1 signature, no MAC under a key known only to A1 and a3). The only item a3 can decrypt is the ciphertext under pk(a3), but that ciphertext is not signed by A1; anyone can encrypt arbitrary values to pk(a3). Thus an attacker can send a fabricated message 3 to A2, which A2 forwards (signed by A2) to a3, causing a3 to accept the listed data as if originating from A1 even when A1 never ran. This breaks even weak (non-injective) authentication of A1 at a3.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker -> A2: (as if from A1) N7,N5; A2 -> Attacker(A1): {N7,N8,N5,N6}inv(sk(A2)); Attacker crafts message 3 with chosen N10 and hashes and { ... }pk(a3) and sends to A2; A2 -> a3: {...inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3); a3 decrypts and accepts data though A1 absent.
Session 2: Real A1 runs later with A2; a3 cannot distinguish the earlier attacker-induced run from a genuine A1-originated one for the claimed fields.

analysis.justification: A1 never receives from A2 any message containing N10 or the specified hash values; A2's signed messages to A1 are message 2 (N7,N8,N5,N6) and message 6 (A1,N7,N8,N5,hash(N10),N4). Since the claimed data items are not delivered to A1 under A2's authentication, A1 cannot establish agreement with A2 on them. An attacker can also alter A1's outgoing message 3 on the insecure channel (including N10 and the hashes) without affecting A1's later verification of A2's signatures, creating mismatch between what A1 used and what A2/a3 see. Therefore the stated authentication goal is not satisfied.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: N7,N5; A2 -> A1: {N7,N8,N5,N6}inv(sk(A2)); A1 chooses N10 and computes hashes for message 3.
Session 2: Attacker modifies A1->A2 message 3 in transit, changing N10 and related hashes; A2 proceeds and later sends message 6 with only hash(N10) and N4, so A1 has no authenticated evidence that A2 agrees on the full tuple claimed in the goal.

analysis.justification: N9 never appears as a standalone transmitted value, but hash(N9,N10) is sent in the clear in message 3. Under pure Dolev-Yao with an uninterpreted hash, this would not reveal N9; however the goal is secrecy of N9 between A1 and A2, and the protocol provides no explicit protection or freshness mechanism for N9 beyond hashing. If N9 is guessable/low-entropy (modeled as Number but not guaranteed fresh here), the attacker can mount an offline dictionary attack by trying candidates for N9 against observed hash(N9,N10) once N10 is also exposed in message 3. Hence secrecy is not ensured by the protocol structure.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker observes message 3 containing N10 and hash(N9,N10) on the network.
Session 2: Attacker tests candidate values for N9 offline by computing hash(N9_guess,N10_observed) until it matches the observed hash, recovering N9 if it is guessable.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0102

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N8,N7,N5,N6,N4,n9;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] pan;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
HmacKey Hx5a3panA1a3;
HmacKey Hx5A2N7;
SeqNumber SQNx3;
SeqNumber SQNx4;
SeqNumber SQNx5;
SeqNumber SQNx10;
SeqNumber SQNx11;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,n9,inv(pk(A1)),inv(hk(A1)),inv(sk(A1)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,pan(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,n9,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,n9,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,pan(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share pan(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N8,N7;
where A1!=a3, A1!=A2, A1!=a3
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {{N5,SQNx3,A2}inv(sk(A1))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {{A1,SQNx4,N6}inv(sk(A2))}pk(A1)
A1 -> A2: {{N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),SQNx5,A2}inv(sk(A1))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: empty
A1 -> A2: {{N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N8,pan(A1,a3),a3}inv(sk(A1))}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: {{N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N8,pan(A1,a3),a3}inv(sk(A1))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: empty
A2 -> a3: {{a3,SQNx10,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N8}inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: {{N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N4,SQNx11,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {{N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N4,SQNx11,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A1)

GOALS:
1. pan(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. a3 weakly authenticates A1 on pan(A1,a3)
3. A2 authenticates a3 on N4
4. A1 authenticates a3 on N4
5. A1 authenticates A2 on N4
6. a3 authenticates A2 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3)
7. a3 authenticates A1 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3)
8. A1 authenticates a3 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),N4
9. A1 authenticates A2 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3)
10. A2 authenticates A1 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3)
11. A2 authenticates a3 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),N4
12. N5,N6 secret between A1,A2,a3
13. hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3) secret between A1,A2,a3
14. N7 secret between A1,A2
15. N8 secret between A1,A2,a3
16. N4 secret between A1,A2,a3
