Session 1: A1 -> A2: {SigA1(N5,SQNx3,A2)}pk(A2); A2 -> A1: {SigA2(A1,SQNx4,N6)}pk(A1); A1 -> A2: {SigA1(...)}pk(A2); A1 -> A2: {SigA1(...,N8,pan(A1,a3),a3)}pk(a3) (A2 obtains ciphertext carrying pan).
Session 2: Attacker replays A1's ciphertext {SigA1(...,pan(A1,a3),...)}pk(a3) to a3 and induces a3 to complete; A2 remains able to link/use pan-bearing message, violating exclusivity to A1,a3.

analysis.goal: a3 weakly authenticates A1 on pan(A1,a3)
analysis.justification: a3 receives a message encrypted under pk(a3) that contains pan(A1,a3) and is signed with inv(sk(A1)). Under Dolev-Yao, only A1 can produce that signature, so whenever a3 accepts pan(A1,a3) from that message it has evidence of A1’s participation. Weak authentication tolerates replay, and the protocol does not require freshness for this goal. Therefore a3 can weakly authenticate A1 on pan(A1,a3).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: ...; A1 -> A2: {SigA1(...,pan(A1,a3),a3)}pk(a3); A2 -> a3: forwards same ciphertext; a3 verifies SigA1 and extracts pan.
Session 2: Attacker replays the same ciphertext to a3; a3 still verifies SigA1 and accepts pan (allowed under weak authentication).

analysis.justification: A2’s evidence for N4 comes from the message signed by a3 in step 9, but that signature does not bind any A2-generated fresh challenge that a3 must have seen uniquely for this run (SQNx10 is from A2 but not echoed by a3). An attacker can replay an old step-9 message from a3 containing some N4 to A2 in a new run, and A2 will accept N4 as coming from a3 without a matching fresh run at a3. This breaks injective-style authentication on N4 for A2.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run completes; a3 -> A2: {SigA3(...,N4,SQNx11,A2,A1)}pk(A2) (attacker records ciphertext).
Session 2: A2 starts a new run; attacker replays recorded a3->A2 ciphertext; A2 accepts N4 as from a3 though a3 did not participate in Session 2.

analysis.justification: A1 receives N4 only via A2 forwarding the a3-signed message (step 10). Since the a3-signed message lacks any A1-specific fresh challenge from the current run (it includes SQNx11 but that is generated by a3), an attacker can replay an old a3-signed message to A1 (possibly via A2) and make A1 accept N4 without a corresponding fresh run by a3. Thus A1’s authentication of a3 on N4 is vulnerable to replay.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run; attacker stores a3 -> A2: {SigA3(...,N4,SQNx11,A2,A1)}pk(A2) and A2 -> A1: same under pk(A1).
Session 2: Attacker replays stored A2 -> A1 ciphertext (or induces A2 to forward stored a3 message); A1 accepts N4 as from a3 though a3 is not running Session 2.

analysis.justification: A1 never receives a message signed by A2 that contains N4. The only message carrying N4 to A1 is signed by a3 (step 10). Therefore A1 cannot cryptographically attribute N4 to A2; an attacker can deliver the a3-signed N4 message to A1 without any A2 involvement in the current run. This violates authentication of A2 on N4.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3 -> A2: {SigA3(...,N4,...)}pk(A2); A2 -> A1: forwards {SigA3(...,N4,...)}pk(A1).
Session 2: Attacker replays the forwarded ciphertext directly to A1; A1 learns N4 but has no A2 signature on N4, so any belief that A2 sent/endorsed N4 is unfounded.

analysis.justification: a3’s only message from A2 is step 8, signed by A2, but it does not include N8 (it includes N8, yes) and includes values that A2 may have obtained from A1 without being able to validate their origin (notably the HMAC terms and encrypted Hx5* keys). An attacker can replay an old A2->a3 signed message (step 8) into a new context, making a3 accept that A2 agrees on the tuple without a fresh corresponding run by A2. Since no a3-generated nonce is included and echoed, replay breaks authentication on that data.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> a3: {SigA2(a3,SQNx10,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N8)}pk(a3) (attacker records).
Session 2: Attacker replays recorded A2->a3 ciphertext to a3; a3 accepts A2 on the tuple though A2 is not participating in Session 2.

analysis.justification: a3 receives the tuple in a message signed by A1 and encrypted to pk(a3) (step 5/6). Because there is no freshness contribution from a3 (no a3 nonce/challenge included and returned), an attacker can replay an old A1-signed ciphertext to a3 in a new run. a3 will accept that A1 agrees on the tuple even though A1 is not currently running. This breaks strong authentication (agreement) on that data.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: {SigA1(...,N8,...)}pk(a3) (attacker records ciphertext); a3 decrypts and verifies.
Session 2: Attacker replays the same ciphertext to a3; a3 accepts A1 on the tuple without a fresh A1 run.

analysis.justification: A1’s only evidence from a3 is the a3-signed message forwarded at step 10, which includes N4 and the tuple but is replayable and not linked to A1’s current run by an A1-generated fresh challenge. An attacker can replay a previously recorded a3-signed message to A1, causing A1 to accept a3 on the entire data set without a corresponding fresh run at a3. Hence authentication fails due to replay/unknown session binding.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3 -> A2: {SigA3(N5,N6,hmac(...),...,N4,SQNx11,A2,A1)}pk(A2); A2 -> A1: forwards under pk(A1) (attacker records).
Session 2: Attacker replays recorded A2->A1 ciphertext; A1 accepts a3 on tuple+N4 though a3 is absent.

analysis.justification: A1 never receives a message signed by A2 that contains N8 and the HMAC tuple. The only A2-signed message A1 receives is step 2, which contains only (A1,SQNx4,N6). Therefore A1 cannot authenticate A2 on the larger tuple; an attacker can complete the protocol to A1 using replayed/forwarded messages without any A2 signature over those fields. This violates the stated authentication goal.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: {SigA2(A1,SQNx4,N6)}pk(A1) (only binds N6).
Session 2: Attacker replays/relays other messages so A1 has N8,N5 and HMAC terms from its own construction, but still no A2 signature over them; A1 cannot authenticate A2 on the tuple.

analysis.justification: A2’s evidence for the tuple is A1’s signed message in step 3, but it is replayable and not tied to A2’s current run by a fresh A2 challenge included in that signed content (SQNx4 is in step 2 but not included in step 3). An attacker can replay an old step-3 ciphertext to A2 in a new run, making A2 accept that A1 agrees on the tuple without a fresh A1 run. This breaks agreement-style authentication.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: {SigA1(N5,N6,hmac(...),...,SQNx5,A2)}pk(A2) (attacker records).
Session 2: A2 initiates; attacker replays recorded A1->A2 ciphertext; A2 accepts A1 on the tuple though A1 is not participating.

analysis.justification: A2 authenticates a3 based on the a3-signed message in step 9, but that message can be replayed across sessions and is not bound to A2’s current SQNx10 (a3 does not include SQNx10 in its signed response). Therefore an attacker can replay an old a3->A2 message containing N4 and the tuple, causing A2 to accept a3 on those values without a fresh run by a3. This violates authentication on the combined data.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3 -> A2: {SigA3(...,N4,SQNx11,A2,A1)}pk(A2) (attacker records).
Session 2: A2 starts new run; attacker replays recorded a3->A2 ciphertext; A2 accepts a3 on tuple+N4 without a3 participating.

analysis.justification: N5 and N6 appear only inside public-key encryptions to pk(A2), pk(A1), or pk(a3). Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker cannot decrypt these without the corresponding private keys, which are not in attacker knowledge. Although ciphertexts are replayable, replay does not reveal plaintext. Hence confidentiality of N5 and N6 against the attacker holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker observes A1->A2 {SigA1(N5,...)}pk(A2) and A2->A1 {SigA2(...,N6)}pk(A1) but cannot decrypt.
Session 2: Attacker replays observed ciphertexts; still cannot derive N5 or N6.

analysis.justification: Both HMAC values are only transmitted inside public-key encryptions to pk(A2) or pk(a3) or inside the a3-signed message encrypted to pk(A2)/pk(A1). The attacker cannot decrypt these ciphertexts, so it cannot learn the HMAC outputs. Even if the attacker could guess inputs, Dolev-Yao treats HMAC as a constructor and does not allow inversion. Therefore the HMAC terms remain confidential from the attacker.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker records ciphertexts carrying the HMAC terms (steps 3,5/6,9/10) but cannot decrypt.
Session 2: Attacker replays them; still cannot derive either HMAC term.

analysis.justification: N7 is never sent in the clear; only hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7) is transmitted, and the key Hx5A2N7 is itself only sent encrypted under hk(A2). Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker cannot invert HMAC to recover N7, nor decrypt {Hx5A2N7}hk(A2) without inv(hk(A2)). Thus the attacker cannot derive N7 from observed traffic.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker observes hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7) and {Hx5A2N7}hk(A2) inside ciphertexts but cannot extract N7.
Session 2: Attacker replays messages; still cannot compute or learn N7.

analysis.justification: N8 is only transmitted inside the message encrypted to pk(a3) (steps 5/6) and inside the A2->a3 message encrypted to pk(a3) (step 8), and later only within encryptions to pk(A2)/pk(A1). The attacker cannot decrypt any of these without private keys. Therefore N8 remains confidential from the attacker.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker records A1/A2 messages containing N8 encrypted to pk(a3) and later encrypted to pk(A2)/pk(A1) but cannot decrypt.
Session 2: Attacker replays ciphertexts; still cannot derive N8.

analysis.justification: N4 appears only in the a3-signed message that is encrypted to pk(A2) and then forwarded encrypted to pk(A1). Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker cannot decrypt either ciphertext without inv(pk(A2)) or inv(pk(A1)). Replay does not reveal N4. Hence N4 remains confidential from the attacker.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Attacker observes a3->A2 {SigA3(...,N4,...)}pk(A2) and A2->A1 {SigA3(...,N4,...)}pk(A1) but cannot decrypt.
Session 2: Attacker replays these ciphertexts; still cannot derive N4.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0103

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1;
Agent A2,a3;
Number N8,N7,N5,N6,N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* Number] pan;
Number empty;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] sk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] hk;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Function [Untyped -> Hash] hash;
Function [Untyped,HmacKey -> Hmac] hmac;
HmacKey Hx5a3panA1a3;
HmacKey Hx5A2N7;
SeqNumber SQNx4;
SeqNumber SQNx10;
SeqNumber SQNx11;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,pan(A1,a3);
A2: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(A2)),inv(hk(A2)),inv(sk(A2)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac;
a3: A1,A2,a3,inv(pk(a3)),inv(hk(a3)),inv(sk(a3)),empty,pk,hk,sk,hash,hmac,pan(A1,a3);
A1,a3 share pan(A1,a3);
A1,A2 agree N8,N7
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {N5}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {A1,SQNx4,N6}inv(sk(A2))
A1 -> A2: {N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3)}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: empty
A1 -> A2: {N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N8,pan(A1,a3)}pk(a3)
A2 -> a3: {N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N8,pan(A1,a3)}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: empty
A2 -> a3: {{a3,SQNx10,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N8}inv(sk(A2))}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: {{N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N4,SQNx11,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2)
A2 -> A1: {N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N4,SQNx11,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))

GOALS:
1. pan(A1,a3) secret between A1,a3
2. a3 authenticates A1 on pan(A1,a3)
3. A2 authenticates a3 on N4
4. A1 authenticates a3 on N4
5. A1 authenticates A2 on N4
6. a3 authenticates A2 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3)
7. a3 authenticates A1 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3)
8. A1 authenticates a3 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),N4
9. A1 authenticates A2 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3)
10. A2 authenticates A1 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3)
11. A2 authenticates a3 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),N4
12. N7 secret between A1,A2
13. N8 secret between A1,A2,a3

analysis.justification: pan(A1,a3) is sent in cleartext within the encrypted message in step 5 (A1 -> A2: {..., pan(A1,a3)}pk(a3)). While encrypted under a3's public key, A2 receives and can forward it to a3. However, the specification states pan(A1,a3) should be secret only between A1 and a3, but A2 learns it. Since A2 is not listed as an authorized party for this secret, this violates the secrecy goal.

analysis.justification: a3 receives pan(A1,a3) encrypted under its public key in step 6, forwarded by A2. The value originates from A1 in step 5. analysis.justification: pan(A1,a3) is sent in clear inside the message encrypted to pk(a3) (A1->A2 and A2->a3). Since a3 can decrypt, secrecy is fine w.r.t. the network attacker, but A2 can also learn pan because it is explicitly listed in A2's final received signature from a3? More directly, A1 includes pan(A1,a3) in the ciphertext to pk(a3) that A2 forwards; if A2 is honest it cannot decrypt, but the goal says secret between A1 and a3 only, excluding A2. Yet a3 later signs a message to A2 that does not include pan, so A2 still cannot derive pan. However, pan is declared as a shared secret between A1 and a3, but it is also used as an HMAC key input and appears as plaintext in the pk(a3) ciphertext; if the attacker compromises a3 (allowed as agent corruption is not stated) then pan is revealed.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I intercepts A1->A2 {N5}pk(A2); forwards; observes later A1->A2 ciphertext to pk(A2) and pk(a3) messages but cannot decrypt; cannot derive pan.
Session 2: I replays A1->A2 message 5 to A2 and forwards to a3; still cannot decrypt pk(a3) and learns nothing about pan.

analysis.justification: a3 never receives any message signed by A1, nor any value that only A1 could have produced. The only evidence about A1 is the inclusion of A1 as an identifier inside A2's signature (A2->a3 message 8) and inside a3's own signed response (message 9), which is circular. An attacker can start a run with A2 using any initiator identity A1 (since A1 is not cryptographically bound to the first ciphertext {N5}pk(A2)), then A2 will sign data naming A1 and send it to a3. Thus a3 may believe it is interacting with A1 on pan(A1,a3) without A1 participating.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->A2: {N5i}pk(A2) pretending to be A1; A2->I: {A1,SQNx4,N6}inv(sk(A2)); I->A2: {N5i,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3)}pk(A2) (replay from prior run or crafted if available); A2 forwards to a3 and signs naming A1.
Session 2: Honest A1 does not run; a3 still completes with A2 based on A2's signature and attributes pan(A1,a3) to A1.

analysis.justification: A2 accepts N4 only in the final message 9, which is encrypted to pk(A2) and contains a signature under inv(sk(a3)) over N4 and the session context (N5,N6,...,SQNx11,A2,A1). The attacker cannot forge a3's signature, and replay across sessions is constrained by inclusion of N5 and N6 that are tied to A2's run (N6 freshly chosen by A2 and signed earlier). Therefore A2's belief that a3 generated N4 is well-founded.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run; a3->A2 sends {{...,N4,SQNx11,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2); A2 verifies signature and accepts N4.
Session 2: I replays Session 1's message 9 to A2 in a new run with different N6; verification fails due to mismatched N5/N6 context.

analysis.justification: A1 receives N4 only via A2 in message 10: { ... ,N4,SQNx11,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3)). This is a3's signature, so origin is a3, but A1 has no guarantee that this N4 corresponds to A1's own run because A1 never sees SQNx11 earlier and does not contribute a fresh value that is included in a3's signature besides N5/N6 which A1 sent but could be replayed. An attacker can replay an old a3-signed message 10 to A1, causing A1 to accept an old N4 without a fresh corresponding a3 run (non-injective/injective failure).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest completion yields A2->A1: {...,N4_old,SQNx11_old,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3)); I records it.
Session 2: A1 starts a new run (new N5_new); I blocks progress and replays the recorded a3 signature to A1; A1 accepts N4_old as from a3 without a matching fresh a3 run.

analysis.justification: N4 is generated and signed by a3, not by A2. A1 only receives N4 inside a3's signature forwarded by A2, but there is no A2 signature or MAC binding N4 to A2's participation for A1. An attacker can deliver the a3-signed blob to A1 without A2 being the sender (or with A2 not participating in the current run), so A1 cannot authenticate A2 on N4.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run; I records A2->A1 message 10 containing a3's signature with N4.
Session 2: I sends the recorded signed message directly to A1 (spoofing A2 on the network); A1 verifies a3's signature and accepts N4 but this does not authenticate A2.

analysis.justification: a3 receives A2's signature in message 8: { {a3,SQNx10,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),{Hx5A2N7}hk(A2),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),{Hx5a3panA1a3}hk(a3),N8}inv(sk(A2)) }pk(a3). This binds A2 to the listed data items, including N8 and the two HMAC values. The attacker cannot forge A2's signature, and replay is limited because a3 can treat SQNx10 as freshness/uniqueness for A2's run (even if not explicitly checked, the signature still authenticates origin).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest A2->a3 message 8 is delivered; a3 decrypts and verifies A2 signature over the exact tuple including N8,N5,N6 and both HMACs.
Session 2: I replays message 8; a3 may detect duplicate SQNx10 or accept as a replay, but in either case the data is still authenticated as originating from A2 (agreement may be non-injective but authentication of origin holds).

analysis.justification: a3 never verifies any A1 signature or MAC under a key shared with A1 over the listed tuple. The only A1-related secret is pan(A1,a3), but the HMAC term is hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3) where the key is pan and the message is Hx5a3panA1a3; this does not authenticate A1 because a3 itself knows pan and can compute the same HMAC, and the value Hx5a3panA1a3 is not shown to be freshly chosen by A1. Therefore a3 can be convinced of A1's involvement without A1 running.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I initiates with A2 and causes A2 to send message 8 to a3 containing the tuple and naming A1; a3 accepts based on A2's signature.
Session 2: No A1 run occurs; a3 still completes and believes A1 is associated with the tuple because nothing cryptographically proves A1 generated it.

analysis.justification: A1's only evidence from a3 is the a3 signature forwarded in message 10, which includes N4 and the tuple. However, A1 cannot ensure freshness or injectivity: an attacker can replay an old a3-signed message 10 to A1 in a new run, and A1 will accept it as authentic for the current session values if N5/N6 match or if A1 does not properly link them. Since A1 never receives N8 in a3's signature directly from a3 (only via A2 forwarding), replay across sessions can break agreement on the intended run.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest completion; I records A2->A1 message 10 with a3 signature over (N5_1,N6_1,...,N4_1).
Session 2: A1 repeats a run reusing N5_1 (or attacker forces reuse by replaying A1's first message context); I replays the recorded message 10; A1 accepts without a fresh a3 run.

analysis.justification: A1 receives only one A2-signed message: message 2 is A2's signature over (A1,SQNx4,N6) and does not cover N5, N8, or the HMAC values. The later data tuple is never signed by A2 to A1; it is only present in a3's signature (message 10). Hence A1 has no cryptographic evidence that A2 agrees on that tuple, so an attacker can mix-and-match values and still have A1 accept.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 gets A2->A1 {A1,SQNx4,N6}inv(sk(A2)); later gets a3-signed tuple via A2.
Session 2: I replays message 2 from an old run to A1 to fix N6, then delivers a different a3-signed tuple (from another run) via spoofed A2; A1 cannot detect mismatch because A2 never signed the tuple to A1.

analysis.justification: A2 never receives any A1 signature or MAC under a key known only to A1 and A2 over the listed tuple. The only A1-originating message is {N5}pk(A2), which is anonymous and does not bind A1 to N5. Consequently, an attacker can initiate the protocol with A2 and drive it to completion (with help of a3) while A2 believes the tuple is associated with A1, even if A1 never participated.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->A2 sends {N5i}pk(A2) as fake initiator; A2 responds and proceeds; later receives a3 signature message 9 naming A1.
Session 2: Honest A1 is idle; A2 still completes and attributes the tuple (including N8,N5i,N6, HMACs) to A1 without cryptographic proof.

analysis.justification: A2 receives message 9 encrypted to pk(A2) containing a3's signature over exactly the tuple plus N4 and identifiers (SQNx11,A2,A1). The attacker cannot forge this signature, and the inclusion of N5 and N6 ties it to A2's run (N6 is chosen by A2 and earlier signed by A2, then carried through to a3). Therefore A2 can authenticate that a3 generated N4 together with the listed data.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run; a3->A2 sends signed tuple including N4; A2 verifies and accepts.
Session 2: I attempts to substitute N4' by modifying ciphertext; fails because signature verification under sk(a3) breaks.

analysis.justification: N7 is used as an HMAC key in hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7) and this HMAC value is sent to a3 inside messages encrypted to pk(a3). While this does not reveal N7 under Dolev-Yao, the goal states secrecy strictly between A1 and A2, excluding a3. Since a3 receives hmac(N7,...) and also receives {Hx5A2N7}hk(A2) but cannot decrypt that, it still learns a function of N7. In symbolic secrecy, revealing hmac(N7,known) typically counts as leaking a term derived from N7, violating strict secrecy of N7 as an atomic secret.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2->a3 message 8 delivers hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7) to a3; attacker observing cannot derive N7 but a3 learns a derived value.
Session 2: Repetition gives a3 additional hmac(N7,...) values, further distinguishing N7-related information from being confined to A1,A2.

analysis.justification: N8 appears only inside messages encrypted to pk(a3) (messages 5,6,8) and inside A2's signature to a3 which is itself encrypted to pk(a3). The network attacker cannot decrypt pk(a3) ciphertexts and thus cannot learn N8. A2 does not receive N8 in plaintext outside of pk(a3) encryption, so the only principals learning N8 are within the intended set.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I observes A1->A2 and A2->a3 pk(a3)-encrypted messages containing N8 but cannot decrypt; N8 remains unknown to I.
Session 2: I replays pk(a3)-encrypted message containing N8; still cannot decrypt and gains no knowledge of N8.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

Since the attacker controls the network, they can intercept these values during the insecure agreement phase before the protocol even begins. The subsequent encryption doesn't restore secrecy for values already compromised.

analysis.justification: The symmetric key shk(A1,A2) is pre-shared between A1 and A2 according to the Knowledge section. It is never transmitted in plaintext during the protocol execution - it's only used as an encryption key in messages 1, 2, and 5. Under Dolev-Yao assumptions without cryptographic breaks, an attacker cannot derive the key from encrypted messages without already knowing it. The protocol doesn't leak the key through any other means.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: {|N5|}shk(A1,A2); A2 -> A1: {|N3|}shk(A1,A2); A1 -> A2: n6; A2 -> A1: prova2(A1,N5); A1 -> A2: {|N5,N3|}shk(A1,A2)
Session 2: Same message pattern with different nonce values, all symmetric encryptions use the same pre-shared key shk(A1,A2)

analysis.justification: N5 and N3 are stated to be insecurely agreed, which means the attacker can know them as initial knowledge (or learn them without protection). Even though N5 and N3 are later sent under the shared symmetric key, secrecy is already lost if the attacker knows them beforehand. Additionally, n6 and prova2(A1,N5) are sent in the clear, and if N5 is attacker-known then prova2(A1,N5) provides no secrecy restoration. Therefore the attacker can distinguish executions based on N5/N3 values and can derive them.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I already knows N5,N3 from the insecure agreement assumption; observes A1->A2 {|N5|}shk, A2->A1 {|N3|}shk, A1->A2 n6, A2->A1 prova2(A1,N5), A1->A2 {|N5,N3|}shk; secrecy of N5,N3 already broken.
Session 2: Same protocol run with different N5',N3'; I knows N5',N3' from insecure agreement and can distinguish Session 2 from Session 1 by these values, violating secrecy between A1,A2.

analysis.justification: The shared key shk(A1,A2) is never transmitted in any message, and all ciphertexts using it do not reveal the key under Dolev-Yao assumptions. Knowing N5 and N3 (even if attacker-known) does not allow deriving the encryption key from ciphertexts. The cleartext messages n6 and prova2(A1,N5) also do not contain shk(A1,A2). Thus there is no symbolic derivation of shk(A1,A2) by the attacker from the observed traffic.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I intercepts all messages: {|N5|}shk, {|N3|}shk, n6, prova2(A1,N5), {|N5,N3|}shk; cannot derive shk(A1,A2) because it is not sent and symmetric encryption is perfect.
Session 2: I repeats interception in a second run (possibly with different N5,N3); still cannot derive shk(A1,A2) from any combination of observed ciphertexts and known plaintexts.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

Under Dolev-Yao assumptions, the symmetric encryption is perfect and reveals no information about N3. Replays do not help the attacker learn N3, only to resend the same ciphertext. Thus confidentiality of N3 against the network attacker holds.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: {|N3_1|}shk(A1,A2); attacker intercepts/replays but cannot decrypt.
Session 2: A1 -> A2: {|N3_2|}shk(A1,A2); attacker intercepts/replays but cannot decrypt.

analysis.goal: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3
analysis.justification: A2 accepts {|N3|}shk(A1,A2) as evidence of A1, but the message has no freshness or session binding. An attacker can replay an old ciphertext from a previous run, causing A2 to believe A1 sent N3 in the current run even if A1 is not participating. This breaks weak (non-injective) authentication because A2’s acceptance does not imply a matching, recent A1 run for that N3.

Session 1: A1 -> A2: {|N3_1|}shk(A1,A2); attacker records ciphertext C1.
Session 2: attacker -> A2: C1 (replay of {|N3_1|}shk(A1,A2)); A2 decrypts and believes A1 sent N3_1 in Session 2 though A1 did not run.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;

A1: A1,A2,shk(A1,A2);
A2: A2,A1,shk(A1,A2);
A1,A2 share shk(A1,A2)
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {|N3|}shk(A1,A2)

GOALS:
1. A1 ->* A2: N3

analysis.justification: The protocol uses symmetric encryption with a pre-shared key shk(A1,A2) that is only known to A1 and A2. Under Dolev-Yao assumptions, without knowledge of shk(A1,A2), an attacker cannot decrypt {|N3|}shk(A1,A2) to learn N3. The channel notation A1 -> A2 (without asterisks) indicates transmission occurs over an insecure channel, but the encryption provides confidentiality for N3. Since the key is properly distributed and never transmitted, N3 remains secret from eavesdroppers.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 generates fresh N3_1, computes {|N3_1|}shk(A1,A2), sends to A2. A2 receives, decrypts with shk(A1,A2), obtains N3_1.
Session 2: A1 generates fresh N3_2, computes {|N3_2|}shk(A1,A2), sends to A2. A2 receives, decrypts with shk(A1,A2), obtains N3_2.

analysis.justification: N3 is only sent once and is protected by symmetric encryption under shk(A1,A2). Under Dolev-Yao assumptions, the attacker can intercept and replay {|N3|}shk(A1,A2) but cannot decrypt it without knowing shk(A1,A2). The initial knowledge does not give the attacker the shared key, and no other messages leak it. Therefore confidentiality of N3 against the network attacker holds for this protocol body.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: {|N3_1|}shk(A1,A2) (attacker intercepts/records but cannot decrypt).
Session 2: A1 -> A2: {|N3_2|}shk(A1,A2) (attacker may replay Session 1 ciphertext; still cannot learn N3_1 or N3_2).

The signature {A2,N3}inv(sk(A1)) reveals N3 in the clear within the signed message structure. Since signatures don't provide confidentiality, an attacker observing the message can extract N3. The protocol sends sk(A1) only in the last message, after A2 already responded, so A2’s acceptance cannot be tied to a verified A1 signature. An attacker can replay or fabricate the first message to trigger A2’s response without any corresponding run of A1 for that N3. Thus weak authentication of A1 on N3 at A2 fails.

Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker can intercept the first message and directly learn N3. Therefore N3 is not confidential.

Since a3 is also a legitimate participant who shares sk(a3) with A1, a3 can decrypt the secure channel messages. More critically, N4 is not encrypted separately and is sent in plaintext within the secure channel, making it visible to anyone who can access that channel.

An attacker can start a run with A2 using an attacker-chosen N6 while claiming to be A1, then relay A2's response to A1 to obtain the needed signed token from a3, and finally forward it to A2. A2 then completes believing it talked to A1 on N6, although A1 never initiated the run with A2 and N6 was attacker-chosen. This breaks agreement/authentication of A1 to A2 on N6.

Even if A1 had a correct run up to sending the signed token to A2, A1 cannot distinguish a genuine A2 response from an attacker-forged one. Therefore A1 can be made to accept an N4 that did not originate from A2, violating authentication of A2 on N4.

Additionally, even ignoring that, N4 is not protected by encryption or signature in the message term itself, so any exposure of the channel endpoint reveals it directly. Hence the attacker can learn N4, violating secrecy between A2 and A1.
analysis.two_session_trace:

PROTO_0113

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Number N3;
Function [Number -> Number] f;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,f;
A2: A2,A1,f
Actions:
A1 -> A2: f(N3)

GOALS:
1. A1 -> A2: f(N3)

analysis.justification: The protocol consists of a single message transmission from A1 to A2 containing f(N3). Since the channel is insecure (->), an attacker can intercept and read the message. However, the goal only specifies message transmission, not confidentiality or authentication. Under Dolev-Yao assumptions, the attacker cannot compute N3 from f(N3) without knowing the function f's inverse, which isn't provided. The message transmission occurs as specified in the protocol.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 generates N3, computes f(N3), sends to A2. A2 receives f(N3).
Session 2: A1 generates new N3', computes f(N3'), sends to A2. A2 receives f(N3').

analysis.justification: The only message is sent over an insecure channel, so the attacker can intercept, modify, drop, or replay it. There is no encryption, MAC, signature, or authenticated/secure channel marker, so A2 has no way to verify the origin or integrity of f(N3). Thus an attacker can impersonate A1 to A2 by sending any value of the form f(X) (or even a different term), and A2 cannot distinguish it from a genuine A1 message. If the goal is confidentiality of f(N3), it also fails because the attacker learns the plaintext directly from the network.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: f(N3) ; I intercepts and blocks it.
Session 2: I(A1) -> A2: f(N3') (or replay f(N3) from Session 1) ; A2 accepts as if from A1.

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0114

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,A3;
Number N4;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,A3,pk(A3),pk(A2);
A2: A2,A1,inv(pk(A2)),pk(A2);
A3: inv(pk(A3)),pk(A3);
A1,A2 share pk(A2)
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {N4,{N4}pk(A3),{N4}pk(A3)}pk(A2)

GOALS:
1. A1 -> A2: N4

analysis.justification: The protocol sends N4 encrypted under pk(A2), which A2 can decrypt using inv(pk(A2)). However, the message also contains {N4}pk(A3) twice, which reveals N4 to A3. Since A3 is not a trusted participant in the A1->A2 communication, and A3 possesses inv(pk(A3)), A3 can decrypt {N4}pk(A3) to learn N4. This violates the confidentiality goal of N4 between A1 and A2 because an unauthorized agent (A3) can obtain the secret.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 generates N4=123, sends {123,{123}pk(A3),{123}pk(A3)}pk(A2) to A2. A2 decrypts with inv(pk(A2)) and obtains N4=123.
Session 2: A3 intercepts the message from Session 1, extracts {123}pk(A3), decrypts with inv(pk(A3)) to learn N4=123, then forwards the original message to A2.

analysis.justification: The only occurrence of N4 on the network is inside {N4,{N4}pk(A3),{N4}pk(A3)}pk(A2), which is encrypted under pk(A2). Under Dolev-Yao, the attacker cannot decrypt this without inv(pk(A2)), which is only known to A2. The inner encryptions under pk(A3) do not help the attacker because they also require inv(pk(A3)) to open. Therefore the attacker cannot derive N4 from intercepted traffic, satisfying confidentiality of N4 in transit from A1 to A2.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: {N4,{N4}pk(A3),{N4}pk(A3)}pk(A2); attacker intercepts but cannot decrypt without inv(pk(A2)).
Session 2: A1 -> A2: {N4',{N4'}pk(A3),{N4'}pk(A3)}pk(A2); attacker replays Session 1 ciphertext or intercepts Session 2, still cannot derive N4 or N4'.

Function [Number,Number,SymmetricKey -> SymmetricKey] clientK,serverK;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,sk(A1),inv(sk(A1)),hash,clientK,serverK,prf,sk(a3);
A2: A2,a3,sk(a3),pk(A2),inv(pk(A2)),hash,serverK,clientK,prf,n10;

A1,a3 share sk(a3);
A2,a3 share sk(a3),sk(a3);
a3,A2 share sk(a3);
A2,a3 share sk(a3)
Actions:

A2 -> a3: n10
a3 -> A1: {A1,sk(A1)}inv(sk(a3))
A1 -> A2: A1,N4,N9,N6

An attacker could act as a man-in-the-middle, forwarding messages between A1 and A2 while learning the session key, since A1 cannot verify A2's fresh contribution to the key derivation.

analysis.justification: prf(N8,N4,N5) appears to remain secret between A1 and A2 under Dolev-Yao assumptions. The key is derived from nonces N4,N5,N8 that are exchanged encrypted under pk(A2) or included in authenticated messages. While authentication flaws exist, an attacker cannot compute prf(N8,N4,N5) without learning all three nonces, and N8 is protected by public-key encryption to A2's public key. However, confidence is moderate due to potential indirect leakage through the hash values.
analysis.two_session_trace: Session 1: A1 and A2 complete protocol normally, deriving prf(N8,N4,N5). Session 2: Attacker attempts to learn the key by intercepting messages but cannot decrypt {N8}pk(A2) without A2's private key or compute the PRF without all nonces.

analysis.justification: A2’s only evidence about prf(N8,N4,N5) comes from the final encrypted hash under serverK(N4,N5,prf(...)), but A2 never receives any A1-origin authenticator that binds prf to A1 in a way A2 can verify. The A1 signature {hash(N5,A2,N8)}inv(sk(A1)) is not checked by A2 because A2 is not given sk(A1) in its knowledge, and the remaining items are either a3’s signature or ciphertexts under pk(A2). An attacker can replay A1’s last message from an old run to make A2 accept a prf value without a matching fresh A1 run (non-injective/injective agreement both fail).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3->A2:{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)); A2->a3:n10; a3->A1:{A1,sk(A1)}inv(sk(a3)); A1->A2:A1,N4,N9,N6; A2->A1:N5,N9,N7,{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)); A1->A2:{A1,sk(A1)}inv(sk(a3)),{N8}pk(A2),{hash(N5,A2,N8)}inv(sk(A1)),{|hash(prf(N8,N4,N5),A1,A2,N4,N5,N9,N6,N7,N8)|}clientK(...)
Session 2: I replays to A2 the Session 1 last message from A1 (including {N8}pk(A2) and the clientK-encrypted hash); A2 completes by sending {|hash(prf(N8,N4,N5),A1,A2,N4,N5,N9,N6,N7,N8)|}serverK(...) believing it matches a fresh A1 run, though no new A1 run occurred.

analysis.justification: A1 accepts A2 based on receiving the final ciphertext under serverK(N4,N5,prf(...)), but there is no explicit A2 signature/MAC over prf or the transcript that A1 can verify. The only signed material A1 sees from the network is a3’s signature {A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)), which authenticates A2’s public key but not A2’s participation in this run or agreement on prf. An attacker can replay an old final serverK-encrypted hash from a prior run to A1, causing A1 to believe A2 agrees on prf without a corresponding A2 run (replay breaks agreement).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run completes: ... A2->A1 sends {|hash(prf(N8,N4,N5),A1,A2,N4,N5,N9,N6,N7,N8)|}serverK(N4,N5,prf(...))
Session 2: I initiates with A1 up to A1 sending its last message; instead of forwarding to A2, I replays the Session 1 final message {|hash(prf(...),A1,A2,...)|}serverK(...) to A1; A1 accepts as if A2 completed this run though A2 did not participate in Session 2.

analysis.justification: The term prf(N8,N4,N5) is never sent in the clear; it only appears inside hashes that are then symmetrically encrypted under clientK/serverK derived from prf itself. The only transmission of N8 is {N8}pk(A2), so the attacker cannot learn N8 without inv(pk(A2)); N4 and N5 are sent in clear but are insufficient to compute prf without N8. Under Dolev-Yao assumptions and uncompromised A2 private key, the attacker cannot derive prf(N8,N4,N5).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I observes A1->A2:A1,N4,N9,N6 and A2->A1:N5,N9,N7,{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)); I also observes A1->A2 includes {N8}pk(A2) and ciphertexts under clientK(...); I cannot decrypt {N8}pk(A2) or the clientK ciphertext.
Session 2: I repeats observation/replay attempts; without inv(pk(A2)) it still cannot obtain N8, hence cannot compute prf(N8,N4,N5) or decrypt serverK/clientK ciphertexts.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

Knowledge:

A2: A2,pk(A2),sk(a3),inv(pk(A2)),{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)),hash,clientK,serverK,prf;
a3: a3,sk(a3),inv(sk(a3))
Actions:
[A1] *-> A2: A1,N4,N9,N6
A2 -> A1: N5,N9,N7,{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3))
A1 -> A2: {N8}pk(A2),hash(N5,A2,N8),{|hash(prf(N8,N4,N5),A1,A2,N4,N5,N9,N6,N7,N8)|}clientK(N4,N5,prf(N8,N4,N5))

analysis.justification: The protocol does not provide secure channel guarantees for prf(N8,N4,N5) from A1 to A2. While A1 sends the term encrypted under clientK(N4,N5,prf(N8,N4,N5)), this key itself depends on the secret prf value. An attacker can replay A1's first message to initiate a parallel session, then use the intercepted encrypted payload from Session 1 in Session 2. Since A2 cannot distinguish between fresh and replayed prf computations in different sessions, the attacker can make A2 accept a prf value from a different session.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 sends A1,N4,N9,N6 to A2. A2 responds with N5,N9,N7,{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)). A1 sends {N8}pk(A2),hash(N5,A2,N8),{|hash(prf(N8,N4,N5),A1,A2,N4,N5,N9,N6,N7,N8)|}clientK(N4,N5,prf(N8,N4,N5)).
Session 2: Attacker replays A1's first message from Session 1. A2 responds with fresh N5',N9,N7',{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)). Attacker forwards {N8}pk(A2) from Session 1 with matching hash(N5',A2,N8), and replays the encrypted payload from Session 1. A2 accepts prf(N8,N4,N5) from Session 1.

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide secure channel guarantees for prf(N8,N4,N5) from A2 to A1. A2's final message contains the prf value encrypted under serverK(N4,N5,prf(N8,N4,N5)), but this key derivation is symmetric to the client key. An attacker can replay messages between parallel sessions, causing A1 to accept a prf value from a different session. The lack of session binding allows the attacker to make A1 believe it's receiving a fresh prf computation when it's actually a replay from another session.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Normal execution completes with A2 sending {|hash(prf(N8,N4,N5),A1,A2,N4,N5,N9,N6,N7,N8)|}serverK(N4,N5,prf(N8,N4,N5)).
Session 2: Attacker initiates parallel session with replayed first message from Session 1. A2 responds with fresh N5',N9,N7',{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)). Attacker manipulates messages to make A1 compute same prf, then replays A2's final message from Session 1. A1 accepts prf(N8,N4,N5) from Session 1.

analysis.justification: A1 never receives any authenticated evidence that A2 actually obtained N8 (and thus prf(N8,N4,N5)). The only A2-to-A1 message that depends on prf is the final ciphertext under serverK(N4,N5,prf), but A1 cannot verify it without already knowing prf and the derived key, so it provides no authentication of A2. An active attacker can impersonate A2 to A1 in Session 1 by replaying a previously observed A2->A1 message 2 (containing N5,N9,N7 and the a3-signed certificate), then drop the rest; A1 will still send prf-derived material believing it is talking to A2. Therefore secure-channel delivery of prf from A1 to A2 is not guaranteed.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I intercepts A1 *-> A2: A1,N4,N9,N6; I replies to A1 with a replayed (N5,N9,N7,{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3))) from Session 2; A1 sends {N8}pk(A2), hash(N5,A2,N8), {|hash(prf(N8,N4,N5),...)|}clientK(...); I blocks delivery to A2.
Session 2: Honest A1 and honest A2 run once; I records A2 -> A1: N5,N9,N7,{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)) for later replay.

analysis.justification: A2 has no way to ensure that the prf it computes is shared with the intended A1 over a secure channel, because A2 never authenticates A1 in message 1 (it is only an authenticated channel from A1 to A2 in the model, but A2’s later acceptance is not bound to a verifiable A1 contribution). An attacker can start a run with A2 using arbitrary values for (A1,N4,N9,N6) and then forward A2’s response to an honest A1 in a different run, causing A2 to complete believing it shares prf with A1 while A1’s run is unrelated. Since the only A2-to-A1 prf-dependent message is encrypted under a key derived from prf, it does not provide A2 with evidence of A1’s participation. Thus the secure-channel goal from A2 to A1 for prf fails under replay/relay.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I initiates with A2 pretending to be A1: sends A1,N4,N9,N6; A2 responds with N5,N9,N7,{A2,pk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)); I forwards this to honest A1 in Session 2; later I forwards A1’s third message to A2 so A2 derives prf and sends final ciphertext, while A1’s run is not securely linked to A2’s belief.
Session 2: Honest A1 starts with A1,N4,N9,N6; receives A2’s response relayed by I; sends {N8}pk(A2), hash(N5,A2,N8), {|hash(prf(...),...)|}clientK(...); I uses it to complete Session 1 with A2.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0118

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4,N5,n6;
Function [Agent -> PublicKey] pk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,n6,pk(a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,n6,pk(a3);
a3: A1,A2,pk(a3),inv(pk(a3)),n6;
A1,A2 share pk(a3)
Actions:
A1 -> a3: {N4}pk(a3)
a3 -> A2: n6
A2 -> a3: {N5}pk(a3)
a3 -> A1: xor(N4,N5)

GOALS:
1. N4 secret between A1,a3
2. N5 secret between A1,A2,a3
3. A1 ->* a3: N4
4. A2 ->* a3: N5

analysis.justification: N4 is encrypted with a3's public key in the first message, which only a3 can decrypt. However, a3 later sends xor(N4,N5) to A1 in plaintext. If A2 learns N5 (which it generates), and intercepts xor(N4,N5), A2 can compute N4 = xor(xor(N4,N5), N5). Since A2 is not in the intended secrecy set {A1,a3}, this violates N4 secrecy between A1 and a3.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 sends {N4_1}pk(a3) to a3. a3 sends n6 to A2. A2 sends {N5_1}pk(a3) to a3. a3 sends xor(N4_1,N5_1) to A1.
Session 2: A2 (acting as attacker) intercepts xor(N4_1,N5_1) from Session 1. Since A2 knows its own N5_1, it computes N4_1 = xor(xor(N4_1,N5_1), N5_1).

analysis.justification: N5 is encrypted with a3's public key, so only a3 can decrypt it. However, a3 sends xor(N4,N5) to A1 in plaintext. If A1 learns N4 (which it generates), and intercepts xor(N4,N5), A1 can compute N5 = xor(xor(N4,N5), N4). Since A1 is in the secrecy set, this might seem okay, but the attacker can exploit this: if the attacker learns N4 from another session (as shown in Goal 1 attack), it can compute N5 from intercepted xor(N4,N5). Thus N5 is not secret from outsiders.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 sends {N4_1}pk(a3) to a3. a3 sends n6 to A2. A2 sends {N5_1}pk(a3) to a3. a3 sends xor(N4_1,N5_1) to A1.
Session 2: Attacker learns N4_1 via Goal 1 attack, intercepts xor(N4_1,N5_1) from Session 1, computes N5_1 = xor(xor(N4_1,N5_1), N4_1).

analysis.justification: A1 sends N4 encrypted under a3's public key {N4}pk(a3). Only a3 possesses the corresponding private key inv(pk(a3)) to decrypt it. Under Dolev-Yao assumptions, without a3's private key, the attacker cannot learn N4 from this encryption. The later leakage via xor(N4,N5) does not affect the confidentiality of this specific channel from A1 to a3, as that occurs after a3 already received N4.

Session 1: A1 sends {N4_1}pk(a3) to a3. Only a3 can decrypt.
Session 2: No attack possible on this channel; attacker cannot decrypt without inv(pk(a3)).

analysis.justification: A2 sends N5 encrypted under a3's public key {N5}pk(a3). Only a3 possesses the corresponding private key inv(pk(a3)) to decrypt it. The later leakage via xor(N4,N5) does not affect the confidentiality of this specific channel from A2 to a3, as that occurs after a3 already received N5.

With N4 known, the attacker computes N5 from xor(N4,N5) in a subsequent run with honest A2. Hence N5 is not confidential against the attacker.",

    }

An active attacker can choose N5 by impersonating A2 to a3 (since a3 does not authenticate A2) and then compute N4 = xor(xor(N4,N5), N5). Thus the attacker learns N4 without breaking encryption, violating secrecy between A1 and a3.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0119

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2;
Number N5,N6;
IntNum V3,V4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,shk(A1,A2);
A2: A2,A1,shk(A1,A2);
A1,A2 share shk(A1,A2)
Actions:
A1 -> A2: {|N5,{|V3|}shk(A1,A2)|}shk(A1,A2)
A2 -> A1: {|N5|}shk(A1,A2),N5,V3

GOALS:
1. N5 secret between A1,A2
2. V3 secret between A1,A2
3. A1 ->* A2: N5
4. A1 ->* A2: V3

}

analysis.justification: N5 is sent in the clear in the second message from A2 to A1, making it visible to any network observer. Since the channel is insecure (->), an attacker can directly read N5 from the second message. The symmetric encryption in the first message does not protect N5's secrecy when it's later exposed in plaintext.

V3 is sent in the clear in the second message from A2 to A1, making it directly observable to any network attacker. The inner encryption of V3 in the first message provides no lasting secrecy since A2 decrypts and retransmits it unprotected. An attacker can simply read V3 from the second message.

N5 is sent from A1 to A2 encrypted under shk(A1,A2) in the first message, providing confidentiality on the insecure channel. The attacker cannot decrypt without the shared key shk(A1,A2). While N5 is later exposed in the second message, that doesn't violate the specific goal of A1 confidentially sending N5 to A2 in the first message.

V3 is sent from A1 to A2 encrypted under shk(A1,A2) within the first message's payload. The double encryption {|V3|}shk(A1,A2) inside {|N5,{|V3|}shk(A1,A2)|}shk(A1,A2) ensures confidentiality on the insecure channel. The attacker cannot access V3 without the shared key, despite its later exposure in the second message.

analysis.justification: A1 receives K6 and A2 only within the signed message {{N4,K6,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) forwarded by A2. This signature uses a3's private signing key inv(sk(a3)), which only a3 possesses. The signature covers N4, which A1 previously sent to A2, binding the response to A1's earlier message. A1 can verify the signature using sk(a3), which it knows. There is no apparent way for an attacker to forge this signature without compromising a3's private key.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 initiates with a3, receives signed A2's public key, sends encrypted nonce to A2. A2 contacts a3, receives signed bundle, forwards to A1. A1 verifies signature and responds with encrypted N5.
Session 2: Same as Session 1 with fresh nonces N4' and N5'.

analysis.justification: A2 receives K6 and A1 within the signed message {{N4,K6,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) directly from a3. This signature uses a3's private signing key, which only a3 possesses. The signature also covers N4, which A2 just sent to a3, binding the response. A2 can verify the signature using sk(a3), which it knows. There is no apparent way for an attacker to forge this signature without compromising a3's private key.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 receives A1's encrypted nonce, forwards N4 to a3, receives and verifies signed bundle from a3.
Session 2: Same as Session 1 with fresh nonce N4'.

analysis.justification: K6 is transmitted encrypted under pk(A2) in message 5 from a3 to A2. While this protects it from eavesdroppers, A2 learns K6. The protocol does not specify that A2 should keep K6 confidential from other parties. Since A2 is a legitimate protocol participant but not necessarily trusted by A1 and a3 to keep K6 secret among all three, the goal 'secret between A1,A2,a3' implies K6 should not be known outside these three. However, the protocol allows A2 to potentially disclose K6 to others without violating any cryptographic assumptions. In a Dolev-Yao model, if A2 is considered potentially malicious or compromised, K6 is not secret from the attacker.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Normal run where A2 learns K6.
Session 2: A2 (acting maliciously or compromised) initiates a session, receives K6', and then sends K6' to an attacker on the network, violating secrecy.

analysis.justification: A1 never receives any message directly from a3 that contains K6; A1 only gets K6 inside a ciphertext encrypted under pk(A1) sent by A2. Thus A1 cannot distinguish whether K6 originated from a3 or was chosen/modified by A2 (or replayed by the attacker from an old run) while still passing the final {|N5|}K6 check. The protocol lacks a3-to-A1 binding of K6 (e.g., a3’s signature on K6 delivered to A1), so agreement of A1 with a3 on K6 is not guaranteed.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: a3 -> A2: {A1,pk(A1)}inv(sk(a3)), {{N4,K6_old,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) (attacker records the inner signed blob for later replay)
Session 2: A1 -> A2: {N4_new,A1}pk(A2); attacker (as network) replays to A2 the recorded {{N4_old,K6_old,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) (or any a3-signed K6 blob from Session 1) and A2 forwards to A1: {{N4_old,K6_old,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3)),N5}pk(A1); A1 accepts K6_old without having evidence it matches a3’s current run with A1

analysis.justification: A2 obtains K6 only by decrypting {{N4,K6,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2), which requires inv(pk(A2)) and yields a3’s signature inv(sk(a3)) over (N4,K6,A2,A1). Since the attacker cannot forge a3’s signature, any accepted K6 is attributable to a3. The inclusion of N4 and identities A2,A1 in the signed data binds K6 to A2’s run and intended peer A1, preventing simple replay across different N4 values.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> a3: A1,A2,{N4_1}pk(a3); a3 -> A2: {A1,pk(A1)}inv(sk(a3)), {{N4_1,K6_1,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) (A2 accepts K6_1 as from a3)
Session 2: attacker replays old a3->A2 ciphertext {{N4_1,K6_1,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) while A2 expects N4_2; after decryption A2 detects mismatch on N4 and cannot complete a consistent run, so authentication on K6 for the current session is not achieved via replay

analysis.justification: K6 is only transmitted inside {{N4,K6,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) and then inside {{N4,K6,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3)),N5}pk(A1), so an attacker without inv(pk(A2)) or inv(pk(A1)) cannot learn K6 from the network. The final message {|N5|}K6 does not reveal K6 under Dolev-Yao assumptions. Unless a private decryption key is compromised (not in the given knowledge), K6 remains confidential to A1, A2, and a3.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: attacker observes a3 -> A2: {{N4_1,K6_1,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3))}pk(A2) but cannot decrypt without inv(pk(A2)); attacker also observes A2 -> A1: {{N4_1,K6_1,A2,A1}inv(sk(a3)),N5_1}pk(A1) but cannot decrypt without inv(pk(A1))
Session 2: attacker replays observed ciphertexts and observes A1 -> A2: {|N5_1|}K6_1; without knowing K6_1, attacker cannot decrypt or derive K6_1 from this symmetric encryption

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0121

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4,n5;
Symmetric_key K6;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,n5,shk(A1,a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,n5,shk(A2,a3);
a3: A1,A2,a3,n5,shk(A1,a3),shk(A2,a3);
A1,a3 share shk(A1,a3);
A2,a3 share shk(A2,a3)
Actions:
A1 -> a3: A1,{|A2,K6|}shk(A1,a3)
a3 -> A2: {|A1,A2,K6|}shk(A2,a3)
A2 -> A1: n5
A1 -> A2: {|N4|}K6

GOALS:
1. N4 secret between A1,A2

analysis.justification: The attacker a3 learns the symmetric key K6 in both message exchanges. In the first message, a3 decrypts {|A2,K6|}shk(A1,a3) using the shared key shk(A1,a3). In the second message, a3 creates {|A1,A2,K6|}shk(A2,a3) using shk(A2,a3), confirming knowledge of K6. When A1 sends {|N4|}K6 to A2, a3 can intercept and decrypt this message using K6 to learn N4. Since a3 is an active attacker with both shared keys, the secrecy of N4 between A1 and A2 is compromised.

A2 sends n5 in the clear and A1’s last message does not bind N4 to n5 or to the session context. An active attacker can replay an old ciphertext {|N4|}K6 from a previous run to A2 in a new run, making A2 accept a value of N4 that the attacker already learned earlier (e.g., by being A2 in that earlier run). Thus, across executions the attacker can distinguish which N4 is used and can know the accepted N4 in the attacked run, violating secrecy between A1 and A2.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I plays A2 with A1. A1->a3: A1,{|A2,K6_1|}shk(A1,a3); a3->I(A2): {|A1,A2,K6_1|}shk(A2,a3) (I knows shk(A2,a3) as A2); I->A1: n5; A1->I: {|N4_1|}K6_1 (I decrypts using K6_1 and learns N4_1).
Session 2: Honest A1 and honest A2 run. A1->a3: A1,{|A2,K6_2|}shk(A1,a3); a3->A2: {|A1,A2,K6_2|}shk(A2,a3); A2->A1: n5; I blocks A1->A2 message and instead replays old {|N4_1|}K6_1 to A2. A2 accepts N4_1, which I already knows, so N4 is not secret between A1 and A2 in this execution.
