PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0122

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,shk(A2,a3);
a3: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3),shk(A2,a3);
A1,a3 share shk(A1,a3);
A2,a3 share shk(A2,a3)
Actions:
A1 -> A2: A1
A2 -> A1: N4
A1 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3)
A2 -> a3: {|A1,A2,{|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3)
a3 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A2,a3)

GOALS:
1. A2 authenticates A1 on N4

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide authentication of A1 to A2 on N4 because A2 cannot verify that the encrypted message {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3) actually originated from A1. The key shk(A1,a3) is shared between A1 and a3, not between A1 and A2. A2 receives this encrypted message but cannot decrypt or verify it directly. A2 forwards it to a3, who can decrypt it and re-encrypt under shk(A2,a3), but this only proves that a3 processed some message containing N4, not that A1 originated it. An attacker could replay an old message from A1 or inject a fabricated message that a3 would process, causing A2 to believe A1 authenticated on N4 when A1 was not involved.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: A1; A2 -> A1: N4_1; A1 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3); A2 -> a3: {|A1,A2,{|A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3); a3 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A2,a3)
Session 2: Attacker replays {|A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3) from Session 1 to A2; A2 -> a3: {|A1,A2,{|A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3); a3 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4_1|}shk(A2,a3) (A2 believes A1 authenticated on N4_1 in Session 2, but A1 did not participate)

analysis.justification: A2 accepts N4 as authenticated from A1 after receiving {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A2,a3) from a3, but this message can be produced by a3 without any participation by A1. Since a3 shares shk(A2,a3), it can encrypt any chosen N4 and identities and send it to A2, making A2 believe A1 was involved. The protocol provides no evidence to A2 that A1 actually saw N4 or generated any response tied to N4 under a key only A1 controls. Thus authentication of A1 on N4 fails (insider/compromised-a3 style attack).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A2 -> A1: N4 (attacker blocks). a3 (acting alone) -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A2,a3). A2 completes believing A1 authenticated on N4.
Session 2: A2 -> A1: N4' (attacker blocks). a3 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4'|}shk(A2,a3). A2 again completes; no A1 run exists in either session.

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0123

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4,n5;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,n5,shk(A1,a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,shk(A2,a3);
a3: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3),shk(A2,a3);
A1,a3 share shk(A1,a3);
A2,a3 share shk(A2,a3)
Actions:
A1 -> A2: A1
A2 -> A1: N4
A1 -> A2: {|n5,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3)
A2 -> a3: {|A1,{|n5,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3)
a3 -> A2: {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3)

GOALS:
1. A2 authenticates A1 on N4

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide authentication of A1 to A2 on N4 because A2 cannot verify that the encrypted message {|n5,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3) actually originated from A1. The key shk(A1,a3) is shared between A1 and a3, not between A1 and A2. A2 receives this encrypted message but cannot decrypt or verify it directly. When A2 forwards it to a3, a3 can decrypt it, but the response from a3 to A2 only contains {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3), which doesn't cryptographically link back to A1's original message. An attacker could replay A1's first message and intercept/modify subsequent messages.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: A1; A2 -> A1: N4_1; A1 -> A2: {|n5,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3); A2 -> a3: {|A1,{|n5,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3); a3 -> A2: {|A1,N4_1|}shk(A2,a3)
Session 2: Attacker replays A1 -> A2: A1; A2 -> Attacker: N4_2; Attacker drops message and uses previously captured {|n5,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3) from Session 1; Attacker -> A2: {|n5,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3); A2 -> a3: {|A1,{|n5,A2,N4_1|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3); a3 -> A2: {|A1,N4_1|}shk(A2,a3) (A2 now believes A1 authenticated on N4_2 but actually has N4_1)

analysis.justification: A2 accepts {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3) as evidence that A1 participated, but this message is produced solely by a3 and is not cryptographically bound to any fresh contribution from A1 that A2 can verify. The attacker can start a run with A2 as if it were A1, obtain N4, and then forward the resulting traffic to a3 using A2’s own key, causing a3 to return the final confirmation for that N4. A2 then concludes A1 is authenticated on N4 even though A1 never ran. This is an impersonation attack enabled by the lack of A1-authenticated data reaching A2.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->A2: A1 ; A2->I: N4 ; I->a3: {|A1,{|n5,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3) (I reuses the ciphertext {|n5,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3) from elsewhere or as an opaque blob) ; a3->I: {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3) ; I->A2: {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3) ; A2 accepts and authenticates A1 on N4.
Session 2: (No A1 run) A1 is idle; attacker repeats Session 1 with a fresh N4' to obtain {|A1,N4'|}shk(A2,a3) from a3 and convince A2 again, showing A2’s belief does not imply A1 participation.

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0124

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,shk(A2,a3);
a3: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3),shk(A2,a3);
A1,a3 share shk(A1,a3);
A2,a3 share shk(A2,a3)
Actions:
A1 -> A2: A1
A2 -> A1: N4
A1 -> A2: {|A1,N4|}shk(A1,a3)
A2 -> a3: {|A1,{|A1,N4|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3)
a3 -> A2: {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3)

GOALS:
1. A2 authenticates A1 on N4

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide authentication of A1 to A2 on N4 because A2 cannot verify that the encrypted message {|A1,N4|}shk(A1,a3) actually originated from A1. The key shk(A1,a3) is shared between A1 and a3, not between A1 and A2. A2 receives this encrypted message but cannot decrypt or verify it directly. Instead, A2 forwards it to a3 wrapped in another encryption, and a3 returns a re-encrypted version under shk(A2,a3). This means A2 only learns that a3 claims A1 sent N4, but cannot distinguish whether the original message came from A1 or from a3 itself. An attacker controlling a3 could fabricate the entire exchange.

A2 accepts {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3) as evidence that A1 participated, but this message is produced by a3, not by A1. Since A2 shares shk(A2,a3) only with a3, any run where a3 is dishonest (or attacker-controlled) lets the attacker make A2 believe A1 authenticated N4 without A1 ever seeing N4. Even if a3 is honest, the protocol only authenticates that a3 forwarded something, not that A1 was the originator. Thus A2's authentication of A1 on N4 is not guaranteed.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I(A1)->A2: A1; A2->I(A1): N4; I(A1) stops (A1 never receives N4).
Session 2: I(a3)->A2: {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3) (attacker uses compromised a3 or its key); A2 accepts and concludes A1 authenticated on N4.

a3 -> A2: {|A1,N4|}shk(A2,a3)

1. A2 authenticates A1 on N4

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide authentication of A1 to A2 on N4 because A2 cannot verify that the encrypted nonce {|N4|}shk(A1,a3) actually originated from A1. The key shk(A1,a3) is shared between A1 and a3, not between A1 and A2. A2 receives this encryption but has no way to decrypt or verify it independently. A malicious agent a3 (who possesses shk(A1,a3)) could generate a valid-looking encryption without A1's participation, and A2 would accept it after forwarding to a3 and receiving confirmation.

Session 1: A1 -> A2: A1; A2 -> A1: N4_1; A1 -> A2: {|N4_1|}shk(A1,a3); A2 -> a3: {|A1,{|N4_1|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3); a3 -> A2: {|A1,N4_1|}shk(A2,a3)

Since a3 knows shk(A2,a3), it can encrypt any chosen pair (A1,N4) and send it to A2, making A2 believe A1 was involved. The protocol provides no evidence to A2 that A1 actually saw N4 or generated {|N4|}shk(A1,a3). Thus agreement on N4 with A1 is not guaranteed.

PROTOCOL_ID:
PROTO_0126

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4,N5;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,shk(A2,a3);
a3: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3),shk(A2,a3);
A1,a3 share shk(A1,a3);
A2,a3 share shk(A2,a3)
Actions:
A1 -> A2: A1
A2 -> A1: N5
A1 -> A2: {|N5|}shk(A1,a3)
A2 -> a3: {|A1,{|N5|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3)
a3 -> A2: {|N5|}shk(A2,a3)

GOALS:
1. A2 authenticates A1 on N5

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide authentication of A1 to A2 on N5 because A2 cannot verify the origin of the encrypted N5. A2 receives {|N5|}shk(A1,a3) from A1, but this encryption uses a key shared between A1 and a3, not between A1 and A2. A2 must forward this to a3 for verification, creating a man-in-the-middle opportunity where an attacker could intercept and replay messages. A2 ultimately receives N5 encrypted under shk(A2,a3) from a3, but this doesn't prove A1 was the original source of N5.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 -> A2: A1; A2 -> A1: N5_1; A1 -> A2: {|N5_1|}shk(A1,a3); A2 -> a3: {|A1,{|N5_1|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3); a3 -> A2: {|N5_1|}shk(A2,a3)
Session 2: Attacker intercepts Session 1's {|N5_1|}shk(A1,a3) and replays it to A2 in a new session where A2 generates N5_2. A2 receives the replayed encryption, forwards it to a3, and a3 returns {|N5_1|}shk(A2,a3), making A2 believe A1 authenticated on N5_1 when A1 never participated in Session 2.

analysis.justification: A2 accepts N5 as authenticated from A1 after receiving {|N5|}shk(A2,a3) from a3, but this only proves that a3 processed a message containing N5, not that A1 actually participated. An attacker can start a run with A2 pretending to be A1, obtain N5 from A2, and then use a separate run with A1 to get {|N5|}shk(A1,a3) without A1 intending to authenticate to A2. The attacker forwards this to A2 (inside the A2->a3 message), causing a3 to return {|N5|}shk(A2,a3) and A2 to complete. Thus A2 can be convinced of A1's involvement on N5 when A1 never ran with A2 on that N5.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->A2: A1
Session 1: A2->I: N5
Session 2: I->A1: (start) A2? / trigger A1 to proceed
Session 2: A1->I: {|N5|}shk(A1,a3)  (I supplies N5 from Session 1 as the challenge)
Session 1: I->A2: {|N5|}shk(A1,a3)
Session 1: A2->a3: {|A1,{|N5|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3)
Session 1: a3->A2: {|N5|}shk(A2,a3)  (A2 completes believing A1 authenticated on N5)
Session 2: (A1 never communicates with A2; A1 only responded to I)

A2 -> a3: {|A1,{|N4|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3)
a3 -> A2: {|N4|}shk(A2,a3)

GOALS:
1. A2 authenticates A1 on N4

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide authentication of A1 to A2 on N4 because A2 cannot verify that the encrypted nonce {|N4|}shk(A1,a3) actually originated from A1. The key shk(A1,a3) is shared between A1 and a3, not between A1 and A2. An attacker controlling a3 can intercept messages and generate valid-looking encrypted nonces. A2 only receives A1's identity in plaintext and an encrypted nonce under a key A2 doesn't possess, then forwards this to a3 for verification. This creates a man-in-the-middle opportunity where a3 (or an attacker impersonating a3) can manipulate the authentication.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1 initiates with A2, sending 'A1'. A2 responds with nonce N4_1. A1 sends {|N4_1|}shk(A1,a3). A2 forwards {|A1,{|N4_1|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3) to a3. a3 returns {|N4_1|}shk(A2,a3).
Session 2: Attacker (controlling a3) initiates with A2, sending 'A1'. A2 responds with nonce N4_2. Attacker intercepts {|N4_2|}shk(A1,a3) from Session 1 and sends it to A2. A2 forwards {|A1,{|N4_2|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3) to a3. a3 returns {|N4_2|}shk(A2,a3), making A2 believe A1 authenticated on N4_2 when A1 never participated.

analysis.justification: A2 accepts N4 after receiving {|N4|}shk(A2,a3) from a3, but this value can be produced by a3 without any participation from A1. The message A2 sends to a3 includes A1 and {|N4|}shk(A1,a3), yet a3 cannot verify that ciphertext was created by A1 (it could be replayed or attacker-supplied), and a3 can still respond with {|N4|}shk(A2,a3) regardless. Thus A2 can be convinced that A1 is involved for nonce N4 even when A1 never ran the protocol. This breaks (non-injective and injective) agreement-style authentication of A1 to A2 on N4.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->A2: A1
Session 1: A2->I: N4
Session 1: I->A2: {|N4|}shk(A1,a3)  (replayed/obtained from elsewhere; not requiring A1 to run now)
Session 1: A2->a3: {|A1,{|N4|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3)
Session 1: a3->A2: {|N4|}shk(A2,a3)  (A2 accepts and authenticates A1 on N4)
Session 2: (No A1 run) I triggers a fresh run again: I->A2: A1; A2->I: N4'; I reuses any available {|N4'|}shk(A1,a3) or replays a matching one; A2->a3: {|A1,{|N4'|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3); a3->A2: {|N4'|}shk(A2,a3) (A2 again accepts without evidence of A1's participation)

PROTO_0128

PROTOCOL_BODY:
Types:
Agent A1,A2,a3;
Number N4;
Function [Agent,Agent ->* SymmetricKey] shk;
Knowledge:
A1: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3);
A2: A2,A1,a3,shk(A2,a3);
a3: A1,A2,a3,shk(A1,a3),shk(A2,a3);
A1,a3 share shk(A1,a3);
A2,a3 share shk(A2,a3)
Actions:
A1 -> A2: A1
A2 -> A1: N4
A1 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3)
A2 -> a3: {|A1,A2,N4,{|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3)
a3 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A2,a3)

GOALS:
1. A2 authenticates A1 on N4

analysis.justification: The protocol fails to provide authentication of A1 to A2 on N4 because A2 cannot verify the origin of the encrypted message {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3). A2 does not possess shk(A1,a3) and must forward it to a3 for verification. However, the attacker can intercept A1's initial message and initiate a parallel session with A2, causing A2 to generate a fresh N4. The attacker can then replay A1's encrypted message from the first session, making A2 believe A1 authenticated on N4 when A1 did not participate in that specific session.
analysis.two_session_trace: Session 1: A1 -> A2: A1 (intercepted by attacker). A2 -> A1: N4' (intercepted). A1 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4'|}shk(A1,a3) (intercepted). Session 2: Attacker -> A2: A1. A2 -> Attacker: N4''. Attacker -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4'|}shk(A1,a3) (replayed from Session 1). A2 -> a3: {|A1,A2,N4'',{|A1,A2,N4'|}shk(A1,a3)|}shk(A2,a3). a3 -> A2: {|A1,A2,N4'|}shk(A2,a3).

analysis.justification: A2 accepts {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A2,a3) from a3 as evidence that A1 participated, but a3 can generate this value without any fresh contribution from A1. The message A2 sends to a3 includes N4 in clear inside the outer encryption, so a3 learns N4 and can respond correctly even if A1 never ran. Thus A2 can complete a run believing it is with A1 on N4 while A1 has not executed the corresponding run, violating authentication.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: I->A2: A1; A2->I: N4; I->a3: {|A1,A2,N4,garbage|}shk(A2,a3); a3->I(A2): {|A1,A2,N4|}shk(A2,a3) (a3 can form it after decrypting and learning N4); A2 accepts as if from A1.
Session 2: Honest A1 run (optional, unrelated): A1->A2: A1; A2->A1: N4'; A1->A2: {|A1,A2,N4'|}shk(A1,a3); (attacker blocks); A2 does not need this to have already accepted in Session 1.

prompt_sent:
You are a security protocol verifier using symbolic reasoning.

You are given:
1) an anonymized Alice-and-Bob / AnB / AnBx protocol body, and
2) an explicit list of goals to analyze.

--- NOTATION REFERENCE ---
Use the following Alice-and-Bob notation when interpreting the protocol:

Keys:
  pk(X)        : public key of X used for encryption
  sk(X)        : public key of X used for signature verification
  inv(pk(X))   : private key of X used for decryption
  inv(sk(X))   : private key of X used for signature
  shk(X,Y)     : pre-shared symmetric key between X and Y

Encryption:
  {|Msg|}K        : symmetric encryption of Msg under key K (e.g. AES, DES)
  {Msg}pk(A)      : asymmetric encryption of Msg under public key of A (e.g. RSA)
  {Msg}inv(sk(A)) : digital signature of Msg using private key of A (e.g. RSA, DSA)

Where:
  Msg is a message, K is a symmetric key, A is an agent

Hashing:
  hash(Msg)    : cryptographic hash of Msg (e.g. MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)
  hmac(K, Msg) : keyed HMAC of Msg under symmetric key K (e.g. HMAC-SHA1, HMAC-SHA2)

--- CHANNELS ---

A -> B: Msg   : Message exchange on an insecure channel
A ->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a confidential channel
A *-> B: Msg  : Message exchange on an authenticated channel
A *->* B: Msg  : Message exchange on a secure channel

--- GOAL DEFINITIONS ---
Authentication (hierarchy, weakest to strongest):
  1. Non-injective Agreement: Adds the requirement that A and B agree on specific data items (such as nonces and keys) and the roles they played.
  2. Injective Agreement: The strongest form — adds a one-to-one relationship between the runs of A and B, preventing replay attacks where B believes multiple runs occurred corresponding to a single run by A. Equivalent to what OFMC/ProVerif verify as injective agreement.

Secrecy:
  Confidentiality: An attacker cannot derive a protected term from intercepted messages. (e.g. A confidentially sends Msg to B, or A ->* B: Msg).
  Secrecy: An attacker cannot distinguish between protocol executions that differ only by their secret inputs (e.g, Msg secret between A,B)


--- RULES ---
- Analyze ONLY the listed goals. Never invent extra goals.
- Use Dolev-Yao attacker assumptions (network control, no cryptographic breaks without keys).
- For EVERY goal, output:
  - status: "attack found" or "no attack"
  - confidence: integer from 1 to 100 reflecting how certain you are in that verdict
  - justification: 2 to 6 sentences
  - two_session_trace: always present, exactly two sessions (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Do not mention filenames, folders, or real protocol names. Refer only to protocol_id.

Return JSON only. No markdown.
Schema:
{{
  "model": "<string>",
  "protocol_id": "<string>",
  "analysis": [
    {{
      "goal_id": <int>,
      "goal": "<goal text>",
      "status": "attack found|no attack",
      "confidence": <int 1-100>,
      "justification": "<100 words>",
      "two_session_trace": "Session 1: ...\nSession 2: ..."
    }}
  ]
}}

analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Normal execution as specified. Attacker observes all messages but cannot decrypt {|A2,N4_1,N5_1,K6_1|}shk(A1,a3), {|A1,N4_1,N5_1,K6_1|}shk(A2,a3), {|N4_1,N5_1|}K6_1, or {|N5_1|}K6_1 without shk(A1,a3), shk(A2,a3), or K6_1.
Session 2: Similar normal execution with different nonces and K6_2. Attacker cannot link K6_1 to K6_2 or derive either key.

analysis.justification: A2 never receives any message that is cryptographically bound to a3 (e.g., under shk(A2,a3)) and that also conveys K6 for A2 to verify. The only K6-carrying component A2 can decrypt in the last-but-one message is {|A2,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A1,a3), but A2 does not know shk(A1,a3), so it cannot validate K6 came from a3. Thus an attacker can replay or substitute the a3->A2 package and drive A2 to accept a K6 without authenticating a3 as the origin tied to A1.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1->A2: A1,N4; A2->A1: A2,N5; A1->A2: {|A1,A2,N4,N5|}shk(A1,a3); A2->a3: (attacker blocks)
Session 2: Attacker replays/forges to A2 a prior a3->A2-looking pair: {|A2,N4,N5,K6'|}shk(A1,a3),{|A1,N4,N5,K6'|}shk(A2,a3); A2 cannot verify the first part and may proceed to send A2->A1: {|A2,N4,N5,K6'|}shk(A1,a3),{|N4,N5|}K6' believing it is from a3

analysis.justification: A1 accepts K6 only after receiving from A2 the ciphertext {|A2,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A1,a3), which A1 can decrypt using shk(A1,a3). Under Dolev-Yao, only A1 and a3 can produce a valid encryption under shk(A1,a3), so the presence of fresh N4,N5 inside binds the message to the current run and to A2’s identity. Even though the message is forwarded by A2, its cryptographic origin is attributable to a3, giving A1 agreement with a3 on (K6,A2).
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: A1->A2: A1,N4; A2->A1: A2,N5; A1->A2: {|A1,A2,N4,N5|}shk(A1,a3); A2->a3: {|A1,A2,N4,N5|}shk(A1,a3),{|A1,A2,N4,N5|}shk(A2,a3); a3->A2: {|A2,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A1,a3),{|A1,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A2,a3); A2->A1: {|A2,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A1,a3),{|N4,N5|}K6
Session 2: Attacker attempts replay of Session 1’s {|A2,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A1,a3) into a new run with fresh N4',N5'; A1 decrypts and detects mismatch (N4,N5 != N4',N5') and rejects, preventing replay-based impersonation of a3

analysis.justification: K6 is sent from A2 to A1 only inside {|A2,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A1,a3), which is confidential to A1 and a3, and inside {|A1,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A2,a3), confidential to A2 and a3. However, the protocol also uses K6 to encrypt {|N4,N5|}K6 and {|N5|}K6 on the public channel; if the attacker can obtain K6 via a3 compromise or by being a3 (allowed as an agent in the model), then these reveal nothing new but K6 itself is no longer secret between A1 and A2. Since a3 learns K6 by design, K6 is not secret strictly between A1 and A2.
analysis.two_session_trace:
Session 1: Honest run: a3 generates/learns K6 and sends to A2: {|A2,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A1,a3),{|A1,N4,N5,K6|}shk(A2,a3); A2 forwards to A1 and uses K6 on-channel
Session 2: Attacker plays role of a3 (or compromises a3’s knowledge) and thus knows K6; attacker trivially derives K6 from its own state, violating 'secret between A1,A2' because a3 is an additional party with access
